```mermaid
    SUBT2 <-->|FORECASTS| SUPSUP
```

---

## 4.4 Diagram 3 — Order Decoupling Point (ODP) Visualization

```mermaid
graph LR
    PUSH1[RAW MATERIAL PUSH BASED ON FORECAST]
    PUSH2[TIER 2 PUSH BASED ON PLANNED ORDERS]
    PUSH3[TIER 1 PUSH BASED ON MONTHLY SCHEDULE]
    PUSHBODY{ODP ORDER DECOUPLING POINT}
    PULL1[OEM ASSEMBLY PULL BASED ON DEALER ORDERS]
    PULL2[DEALER PULL BASED ON CUSTOMER ORDERS]
    PULL3[CUSTOMER CONFIGURES VEHICLE]

    PUSH1 --> PUSH2
    PUSH2 --> PUSH3
    PUSH3 --> PUSHBODY
    PUSHBODY --> PULL1
    PULL1 --> PULL2
    PULL2 --> PULL3

    PUSHBODY -.->|MARK VEHICLE VARIANT| PULL1
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]
**"Differentiate between Tier-1 and Tier-2 automotive suppliers with suitable examples."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Tier-1 Supplier | Tier-2 Supplier |
|--------|----------------|-----------------|
| **Definition** | Direct vendor to OEM; delivers complete modules/systems | Sub-vendor to Tier-1; supplies sub-components |
| **Examples** | Bosch (ABS modules), Denso (HVAC), Motherson Sumi (interiors) | Bharat Forge (forged crankshaft to Tier-1), Sundaram Fasteners (bolts/studs) |
| **Relationship with OEM** | Direct contract, EDI-based, JIS delivery | Indirect — through Tier-1 |
| **Decision Rights** | Co-design, capacity planning, PPAP submission | Manufactures to Tier-1 drawing specs |

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Expected]
**"What is the Order Decoupling Point (ODP) in the automotive supply chain? Where is it typically located?"**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **Order Decoupling Point (ODP)** is the position in the supply chain at which the **push-based forecast-driven production** ends and **pull-based customer-order-driven production** begins. In the automotive industry, the ODP is typically located at the **final assembly line (or the paint shop → trim/chassis line transition)** of the OEM. Upstream of the ODP, components are built against forecasts (generic, variant-unspecified); downstream of the ODP, vehicles are configured, sequenced, and built to specific customer/dealer orders (e.g., specific color, engine, transmission combination).

---

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU ESE Part B questions offer **internal choice** between two 14-mark questions. The model solutions below mirror actual board valuation patterns.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]**

> **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **structure of an automotive supply chain**, clearly identifying all five major components. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Discuss the **three flows** (material, information, financial) in the automotive supply chain with a real-world Indian example. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Introduction (1 mark):**
The automotive supply chain is a **multi-tier, multi-echelon, hybrid push-pull network** that converts raw materials into finished vehicles and delivers them to end customers. It consists of five major structural components.

**Five Components (4 marks):**

1. **Suppliers (Tiered):** Tier-1 (module integrators), Tier-2 (sub-component makers), Tier-3 (raw material providers).
2. **Manufacturer (OEM):** Performs design, engineering, final assembly, quality, and brand management (e.g., Maruti Suzuki, Tata Motors).
3. **Distributors:** Captive stockyards or 3PL providers handling bulk finished-vehicle transport to regional hubs.
4. **Retailers (Dealers):** Authorized franchisees conducting sales, financing, and after-sales service.
5. **Customers:** B2C individual buyers and B2B fleet/corporate/export buyers.

**Block Diagram (2 marks):**
Refer to **Section 4.3 — Diagram 2** for the full Mermaid block flow architecture. The chain is depicted as:

> Raw material sources $\rightarrow$ Tier-2 $\rightarrow$ Tier-1 $\rightarrow$ OEM $\rightarrow$ Distributor $\rightarrow$ Retailer $\rightarrow$ Customer

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Material Flow (2.5 marks):**
Physical movement of parts from Tier-3 to Tier-1, modules to OEM, and finished vehicles to dealers. In India, Maruti Suzuki sources steel from Tata Steel (Tier-3), stamped panels from suppliers in Manesar (Tier-2), and seats from Motherson Sumi (Tier-1) — all delivered Just-in-Sequence (JIS) to the assembly line. Finished Swift/Baleno vehicles are dispatched via car-carrier trailers to 200+ regional stockyards.

**Information Flow (2.5 marks):**
Bi-directional. Demand forecasts flow upstream (customer $\rightarrow$ dealer $\rightarrow$ OEM $\rightarrow$ Tier-1 $\rightarrow$ Tier-2 $\rightarrow$ Tier-3). Actual purchase orders, dispatch notifications (ASN), and CRM data flow downstream. Modern OEMs use EDI, VMI (Vendor Managed Inventory), and cloud-based S&OP platforms.

**Financial Flow (2 marks):**
Funds move largely **upstream-to-downstream** (customer pays dealer, dealer pays OEM, OEM pays Tier-1, etc.) and partially **downstream-to-upstream** in the form of warranty recoveries, recall costs, and supplier development investments. Maruti's strong working-capital management has historically kept its **cash-to-cash cycle** under 30 days.

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model]**

> **(a)** Explain the **tier structure of suppliers** in the automotive industry. Why is this structure considered a source of both efficiency and risk? **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** With suitable examples, describe the concept of the **Order Decoupling Point (ODP)** and explain how it divides the automotive supply chain into push and pull segments. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Tier Structure (4 marks):**
The automotive supplier base is structured in three (sometimes four) tiers:

- **Tier-3 (Raw Materials):** Mines, oil refineries, smelters, silicon fabs. Supply commodity-grade inputs.
- **Tier-2 (Sub-Components):** Process raw materials into engineered parts (e.g., Bharat Forge — forged crankshafts; Sundaram Fasteners — bolts).
- **Tier-1 (Module/System Suppliers):** Deliver complete ready-to-install modules to OEM (e.g., Bosch — ABS, Denso — HVAC, Continental — instrument clusters).

**Efficiency Source (1.5 marks):**
- Specialization at each tier
- Economies of scale
- Allows OEM to focus on core competencies (design, assembly, brand)

**Risk Source (1.5 marks):**
- Single-source dependencies (e.g., 2020–22 semiconductor crisis)
- Bullwhip effect propagation
- Geopolitical exposure (e.g., rare-earth dependency on China)
- Cascading failures (one Tier-2 halt can stop a Tier-1, halting the OEM)

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**ODP Definition (2 marks):**
The **Order Decoupling Point (ODP)** is the position in the supply chain where the production logic shifts from **forecast-driven (push)** to **order-driven (pull)**. It is the boundary at which inventory is no longer built against forecasts but is instead configured against a specific customer/dealer order.

**Push Segment (2.5 marks):**
Upstream of the ODP, all production is **push** — driven by long-range forecasts, master production schedules, and blanket purchase orders. Tier-3 to OEM pre-assembly operations fall here. Inventory is built for variant-generic components (e.g., undifferentiated brake discs, uncolored dashboards).

**Pull Segment (2.5 marks):**
Downstream of the ODP, all production is **pull** — driven by actual customer/dealer orders. The OEM final assembly (e.g., choosing the exact color, engine, transmission) and the dealer delivery process are pull. The vehicle is built to order from this point.

**Example:** In a Maruti Suzuki plant, the ODP is at the **paint shop entry**. Cars before this point are "color-unknown" (push); after the paint shop, the specific color is applied (pull) and the vehicle is sequenced to a specific dealer order.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Confusing Tier-1 with raw material supplier** — Tier-1 is a *module/system* supplier, NOT a raw material vendor. Examiners deduct **2 marks** for this.
> 2. **Treating the chain as purely linear** — KTU expects you to mention **tiered structure, three flows, and ODP**. A simple 1-D arrow chain scores only 50% marks.
> 3. **Missing the three-flows concept** — A surprising number of answers describe only material flow. Examiners insist on **material + information + financial** as a mandatory triad (costs **3 marks**).
> 4. **No Indian/global example** — Generic textbook answers score poorly. Cite **Maruti, Tata, Bosch, Motherson, Denso, Toyota, VW** to gain 1–2 extra marks.
> 5. **Skipping the Mermaid-style diagram** — A hand-drawn or ASCII block diagram is **mandatory** for the 7-mark part (a). No diagram = **−2 marks** minimum.
> 6. **ODP misplacement** — Placing ODP at Tier-1 instead of final assembly is a common error; the correct placement is **at or just before final trim/chassis assembly**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Revision — 60-Second Recall List:**

- ☐ The chain has **5 components**: Suppliers (Tier-1/2/3) → OEM → Distributor → Retailer → Customer
- ☐ **Three flows run in parallel**: Material (downstream), Information (bi-directional), Financial (upstream)
- ☐ **Tier-1 = module supplier** (direct to OEM); **Tier-2 = sub-component**; **Tier-3 = raw material**
- ☐ **Order Decoupling Point (ODP)** is at **final assembly** in automotive
- ☐ **Bullwhip Effect** amplifies demand variance by **4× to 10×** upstream
- ☐ **Push** logic dominates upstream; **Pull** logic dominates downstream of ODP
- ☐ **Tier Multiplier:** a single OEM can have **>10,000 firms** in its extended supply base
- ☐ **Cash-to-Cash Cycle** in automotive = **30–60 days**
- ☐ Always include a **diagram** in 7-mark questions
- ☐ Always cite an **Indian example** (Maruti, Tata, Bosch India, Motherson) for full marks
- ☐ Real-world risks: **single-source dependency**, **geopolitical supply shocks**, **bullwhip amplification**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Supply Chain Models and Frameworks: Overview of Common Models Such as the SCOR Model and Their Application in the Automotive Industry
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT753 - SUPPLY CHAIN MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Supply Chain Models and Frameworks: Overview of Common Models Such as the SCOR Model and Their Application in the Automotive Industry

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Supply Chain Model?

A **Supply Chain Model** is a structured, formal representation of the network of organizations, people, activities, information, and resources involved in moving a product or service from supplier to customer. A **Supply Chain Framework** is the standardized architecture, vocabulary, and methodology used to describe, measure, evaluate, and improve supply chain performance across industries.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> A supply chain model provides a common language and a consistent set of metrics that allow enterprises to communicate supply chain performance objectives, evaluate performance, and identify improvement opportunities.

## 1.2 The SCOR Model (Supply Chain Operations Reference Model)

The **SCOR Model**, developed by the **Supply Chain Council (now part of ASCM – Association for Supply Chain Management)** in 1996, is the most widely accepted cross-industry standard framework for supply chain management.

> [!NOTE]
> **SCOR = Supply Chain Operations Reference Model**
> It links strategy, processes, and performance metrics into a single integrated framework.

**Core SCOR Statement:** The SCOR model describes the business activities a company performs to fulfill customer demand. It provides a process reference model, performance metrics, best practices, and people skills.

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy (The Relay Race)

Imagine a supply chain as a **4 × 100 meters relay race team**:

- **Plan** → The coach studies the opponents, decides the race strategy, and orders the training equipment.
- **Source** → Sourcing the best running shoes, track suits, and energy drinks from vendors.
- **Make** → Training the athletes and putting them in the right lanes.
- **Deliver** → Running the race and crossing the finish line on time.
- **Return** → Returning to the starting block if a baton exchange failed or a medical checkup is needed.
- **Enable** → The support staff, physiotherapists, and nutritionists who make the whole race possible.

Without a common playbook (the **SCOR framework**), every team member would run in different directions. SCOR provides that universal playbook.

## 1.4 Standard Metrics and Terminology

The following **bold metrics** are foundational in the SCOR model:

- **Reliability** → Ability to perform tasks as expected
- **Responsiveness** → Speed at which tasks are performed
- **Agility** → Ability to respond to external influences
- **Costs** → Cost of operating the supply chain
- **Asset Management Efficiency (AM)** → Effectiveness in managing assets

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Quick Recall – The 5 Performance Attributes of SCOR:** **R**eliability, **R**esponsiveness, **A**gility, **C**ost, **A**M (Asset Management) – remember as **"RRACA"**.

## 1.5 Why Study the Automotive Industry Application?

The automotive industry is considered the **"birthplace"** of modern supply chain thinking. Concepts like *Just-In-Time (JIT)*, *Lean Manufacturing*, and *Vendor-Managed Inventory* were pioneered by **Toyota** and later adopted globally. Therefore, automotive supply chains serve as a benchmark for testing supply chain models in the real world.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Six Core Process Types of the SCOR Model

The SCOR model organizes all supply chain activities into **six primary process categories** (P1 to P6):

| Process Code | Process Name | Scope / Key Function |
|--------------|--------------|---------------------|
| **P1** | **Plan** | Demand/supply planning, inventory positioning, distribution planning |
| **P2** | **Source** | Procurement, supplier scheduling, receiving, verification |
| **P3** | **Make** | Manufacturing, packaging, release, testing |
| **P4** | **Deliver** | Order management, warehousing, transportation, invoicing |
| **P5** | **Return** | Reverse logistics for defective or excess products |
| **P6** | **Enable** | Management of supply chain rules, performance, resources |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The processes are executed across **Plan → Source → Make → Deliver → Return** in sequence. **Enable** is a horizontal support process that runs across all the above.

## 2.2 Hierarchical Structure of SCOR (The Four Levels)

SCOR is built as a **four-level hierarchical model**:

- **Level 1 – Strategic (Process Types):** Defines the scope of the supply chain (P1–P6).
- **Level 2 – Tactical (Process Categories):** Configures the supply chain strategy (e.g., Make-to-Stock, Make-to-Order, Engineer-to-Order).
- **Level 3 – Operational (Process Elements):** Detailed step-by-step activities (e.g., "Verify Product", "Consume Product").
- **Level 4 – Implementation (Tasks):** Company-specific tasks that are not defined by SCOR; they are built on prior frameworks (e.g., Six Sigma, TQM, ERP systems).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Key Point:** Levels 1–3 are defined by the SCOR model. Level 4 is **NOT** defined by SCOR – it is left to the implementing company.

## 2.3 The Global Supply Chain Forum (GSCF) Model

Apart from SCOR, KTU syllabus recognizes the **GSCF Model** developed by **Professor Douglas M. Lambert** (Ohio State University). It identifies **8 key supply chain processes**:

1. Customer Relationship Management (CRM)
2. Customer Service Management (CSM)
3. Demand Management (DM)
4. Order Fulfillment (OF)
5. Manufacturing Flow Management (MFM)
6. Supplier Relationship Management (SRM)
7. Procurement and Sourcing (PS)
8. Product Development and Commercialization (PDC)

## 2.4 Other Common Frameworks

- **The Reference Model (OECD / APICS)** – Generic reference for inter-company logistics.
- **Cox's Process Model** – Focuses on 4 supply chain activities (Plan, Source, Make, Deliver).
- **The Leagile Framework** – Combines Lean + Agile paradigms.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Performance Metrics)

The SCOR model uses standardized Level 1 metrics. Below are the most common equations and their meanings:

| Metric Category | Standard Formula | KTU Frequently Asked Variable |
|-----------------|------------------|--------------------------------|
| **Perfect Order Fulfillment (POF)** | $POF = \dfrac{\text{Perfect Orders}}{\text{Total Orders}} \times 100\%$ | Reliability – **most frequently asked** |
| **Order Fulfillment Cycle Time (OFCT)** | $OFCT = \text{Order Entry Date} - \text{Customer Receipt Date}$ | Responsiveness |
| **Upside Supply Chain Flexibility** | $\Delta \text{ Days of supply} / \Delta \text{ Demand \%}$ | Agility |
| **Cash-to-Cash Cycle Time (C2C)** | $C2C = DIO + DSO - DPO$ | Asset Management |
| **Return on Fixed Assets (ROFA)** | $ROFA = \dfrac{\text{EBIT}}{\text{Net Fixed Assets}} \times 100\%$ | Asset Management |
| **Total Supply Chain Management Cost** | $TSCMC = \text{Plan} + \text{Source} + \text{Make} + \text{Deliver} + \text{Return}$ | Cost |
| **Fill Rate (FR)** | $FR = \dfrac{\text{Units Shipped}}{\text{Units Ordered}} \times 100\%$ | Reliability |
| **Inventory Days of Supply (IDS)** | $IDS = \dfrac{\text{Average Inventory}}{\text{COGS} / 365}$ | Asset Management |

> [!TIP]
> **Where:**
> - $DIO$ = Days Inventory Outstanding
> - $DSO$ = Days Sales Outstanding
> - $DPO$ = Days Payable Outstanding
> - $EBIT$ = Earnings Before Interest and Taxes

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

In automotive industry, these metrics are computed using **ERP systems (SAP, Oracle)** and **MES (Manufacturing Execution Systems)**. For example, Toyota computes its **Cash-to-Cash Cycle Time** every 4 hours to align with its just-in-time sequencing.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Computing the Perfect Order Fulfillment (POF) in an Automotive Plant

A major automotive OEM (Original Equipment Manufacturer) records the following data for Quarter 1:

- Total customer orders received: **50,000 cars**
- Orders delivered on time: **46,000**
- Orders delivered complete (no missing parts): **44,000**
- Orders with damage-free documentation: **43,000**
- Orders with correct invoicing: **42,000**

A "perfect order" is one that satisfies **all four** conditions simultaneously (on-time, complete, damage-free, correctly invoiced).

**Step 1:** Identify the common subset. The number of perfect orders = the **minimum** of all four counts.

$$
N_{\text{perfect}} = \min(46000, 44000, 43000, 42000) = 42000
$$

**Step 2:** Apply the POF formula.

$$
POF = \frac{N_{\text{perfect}}}{N_{\text{total}}} \times 100\%
$$

**Step 3:** Substitute the values.

$$
POF = \frac{42000}{50000} \times 100\%
$$

**Step 4:** Compute the result.

$$
POF = 0.84 \times 100\% = 84\%
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The automotive plant achieves a **84% Perfect Order Fulfillment**, which is above the global automotive industry benchmark of **80–85%**.

## 3.2 Derivation: Cash-to-Cash Cycle Time for a Tier-1 Supplier

A Tier-1 automotive supplier (e.g., a brake-system manufacturer) reports:
- Days Inventory Outstanding ($DIO$) = **45 days**
- Days Sales Outstanding ($DSO$) = **60 days**
- Days Payable Outstanding ($DPO$) = **30 days**

**Step 1:** Recall the formula.

$$
C2C = DIO + DSO - DPO
$$

**Step 2:** Substitute the values.

$$
C2C = 45 + 60 - 30
$$

**Step 3:** Compute.

$$
C2C = 105 - 30 = 75 \text{ days}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The supplier's money is locked in operations for 75 days. World-class automotive supply chains target a **C2C $\leq$ 40 days** (similar to Dell and Toyota).

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Major Supply Chain Models

| Feature | SCOR Model | GSCF Model | Cox's Model | Leagile Framework |
|---------|------------|------------|-------------|-------------------|
| **Origin** | Supply Chain Council, 1996 | Lambert et al., 1998 | Cox, 1999 | Naim & Towill, 2001 |
| **Number of Processes** | 6 (P1–P6) | 8 | 4 | 2 (Lean + Agile) |
| **Process Hierarchy** | 4 Levels | 3 Levels | 2 Levels | Conceptual |
| **Performance Metrics** | Built-in (RRACA) | Indirect | None | Indirect |
| **Industry Coverage** | Cross-industry | Generic | Retail/FMCG | Manufacturing |
| **Reverse Logistics** | Yes (P5) | Implicit | No | Yes |
| **Best Suited For** | Automotive, Tech, Defense | Strategy Formulation | Academic Study | Mass Customization |
| **Alignment with KTU Module 1** | **Highly Relevant** | Moderately Relevant | Auxiliary Reference | Emerging Topic |

## 3.4 Application Matrix: SCOR in the Automotive Industry

| SCOR Process | Automotive Use-Case (Real Example) | Key Metric |
|--------------|------------------------------------|------------|
| **P1 – Plan** | Toyota's monthly production schedule aligned with dealer demand forecast | Forecast Accuracy |
| **P2 – Source** | BMW procuring rare-earth magnets from sustainable mines in Australia | Supplier Defect Rate |
| **P3 – Make** | Tesla's Gigafactory producing battery packs | Cycle Time per Vehicle |
| **P4 – Deliver** | Maruti Suzuki's dispatch through India via road/rail mix | On-Time Delivery % |
| **P5 – Return** | Volkswagen's recall management for defective airbags | Reverse Logistics Cost |
| **P6 – Enable** | Ford's global SCOR governance council | SCOR Maturity Level |

## 3.5 Maturity Levels of SCOR Implementation

SCOR provides a **maturity roadmap** that an organization progresses through:

$$
\text{Stage 1: Siloed} \rightarrow \text{Stage 2: Functional} \rightarrow \text{Stage 3: Cross-Functional}
$$

$$
\rightarrow \text{Stage 4: Aligned} \rightarrow \text{Stage 5: Agile/Networked}
$$

> [!TIP]
> Most Indian automotive suppliers (Tier 2/Tier 3) operate at **Stage 2 or 3**. Global OEMs like Toyota operate at **Stage 5**.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1: SCOR Process Flow (P1 to P5 with P6 as Enabler)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[P1 Plan\nDemand and Supply Planning] --> A2[P2 Source\nProcurement and Receiving]
    A2 --> A3[P3 Make\nManufacturing and Testing]
    A3 --> A4[P4 Deliver\nOrder Management and Shipping]
    A4 --> A5[P5 Return\nReverse Logistics]
    A5 --> A2
    A6[P6 Enable\nRules, Resources, Performance] -.-> A1
    A6 -.-> A2
    A6 -.-> A3
    A6 -.-> A4
    A6 -.-> A5
    style A1 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style A2 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style A3 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style A4 fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style A5 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style A6 fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333,color:#000
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2: SCOR Hierarchical Levels (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    L1[Level 1 Strategic\nProcess Types P1 to P6] --> L2
    L2[Level 2 Tactical\nProcess Categories MTO MTS ETO] --> L3
    L3[Level 3 Operational\nProcess Elements Detailed Steps] --> L4
    L4[Level 4 Implementation\nCompany Specific Tasks]
    L4 --> EXT[Implemented via TQM Six Sigma ERP]
    style L1 fill:#F4A460,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style L2 fill:#87CEFA,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style L3 fill:#98FB98,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style L4 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style EXT fill:#D3D3D3,stroke:#333,color:#000
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3: Automotive Supply Chain Application of SCOR

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    OEM[OEM Assembly Plant\ne.g. Maruti Suzuki] -->|P2 Source| T1[Tier 1 Supplier\nBrakes and Engine]
    OEM -->|P2 Source| T2[Tier 2 Supplier\nSub Components]
    T1 -->|P2 Source| T2
    T2 -->|P1 Plan| OEM
    OEM -->|P3 Make| ASSY[Vehicle Assembly Line]
    ASSY -->|P4 Deliver| DC[Distribution Center]
    DC -->|P4 Deliver| DEAL[Dealer Network]
    DEAL -->|P5 Return| OEM
    DEAL -->|P4 Deliver| CUST[End Customer]
    OEM -->|P6 Enable| GOV[Global SCOR Governance]
    style OEM fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000
    style T1 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000
    style T2 fill:#ADD8E6,stroke:#000
    style ASSY fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000
    style DC fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#000
    style DEAL fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000
    style CUST fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#000
    style GOV fill:#D3D3D3,stroke:#000
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram 4: Performance Attribute Hierarchy (RRACA)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    SCOR[SCOR Performance Attributes] --> REL[Reliability]
    SCOR --> RES[Responsiveness]
    SCOR --> AGI[Agility]
    SCOR --> CST[Cost]
    SCOR --> AME[Asset Management Efficiency]
    REL --> POF[Perfect Order Fulfillment]
    RES --> OFCT[Order Fulfillment Cycle Time]
    AGI --> USCF[Upside Supply Chain Flexibility]
    CST --> TSCC[Total Supply Chain Cost]
    AME --> C2C[Cash to Cash Cycle Time]
    style SCOR fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000
    style REL fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000
    style RES fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#000
    style AGI fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000
    style CST fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000
    style AME fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#000
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]
**Define the SCOR model. List its six core processes.**

**Model Answer:**
The **SCOR (Supply Chain Operations Reference) Model** is a process reference model developed by the Supply Chain Council (now ASCM) that provides a standard framework for evaluating and improving supply chain performance.

The **six core processes** are:
1. **P1 – Plan** (Demand and supply planning)
2. **P2 – Source** (Procurement of goods and services)
3. **P3 – Make** (Manufacturing and transformation)
4. **P4 – Deliver** (Order management and distribution)
5. **P5 – Return** (Reverse logistics for defective products)
6. **P6 – Enable** (Management of rules and performance)

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Sample]
**Mention any two common supply chain frameworks other than SCOR.**

**Model Answer:**
1. **GSCF (Global Supply Chain Forum) Model** – Proposed by Douglas Lambert; identifies 8 key processes including CRM, CSM, Demand Management.
2. **Cox's Process Model** – Focuses on 4 processes: Plan, Source, Make, Deliver.

*(Any other valid framework like Leagile or the OECD Reference Model can also be mentioned.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each – Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a)** Explain in detail the **SCOR model** with its four hierarchical levels. Discuss the significance of **Level 4** in the SCOR framework. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Apply the **SCOR framework** to a passenger car manufacturing company. Explain how each of the six SCOR processes (P1–P6) is implemented, citing at least **one real-world automotive example** for each. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for (a):

The **SCOR model** is the most widely adopted supply chain framework that standardizes process terminology, performance metrics, and best practices. It is built in a four-level hierarchical structure:

- **Level 1 – Strategic Level (Process Types):** Defines the scope and strategic objectives of the supply chain using the six process types (P1–P6). At this level, firms identify the type of supply chain (e.g., Make-to-Stock, Make-to-Order).

- **Level 2 – Tactical Level (Process Categories):** Breaks each process type into process categories. For example, P3 (Make) is decomposed into:
  - M3.1 – Make-to-Stock
  - M3.2 – Make-to-Order
  - M3.3 – Engineer-to-Order
  - D3.4 – Assembly-to-Order

- **Level 3 – Operational Level (Process Elements):** Detailed step-by-step activities such as "Verify Product", "Stage Product", "Consume Product".

- **Level 4 – Implementation Level:** Company-specific, customized tasks that implement the Level 3 elements. This level is **not defined by SCOR** itself.

**Significance of Level 4:**
Level 4 bridges SCOR's generic framework with the company's internal systems. It is implemented using:
- ERP systems (e.g., SAP MM/PP)
- Lean tools (Kanban, Heijunka)
- Six Sigma / TQM
- Custom software modules

Without Level 4, SCOR remains an abstract model. Level 4 is where **real-world execution** happens.

---

#### Model Solution for (b):

| SCOR Process | Implementation in a Car Company (e.g., Maruti Suzuki) | Real-world Example |
|--------------|-------------------------------------------------------|--------------------|
| **P1 – Plan** | Monthly rolling forecast using historical dealer data | Aligns production with festive season demand spikes in India |
| **P2 – Source** | Vendor-managed inventory with Tier-1 suppliers | Bosch supplies ABS systems just-in-time to the Gurugram plant |
| **P3 – Make** | Lean assembly lines using Heijunka (level scheduling) | Toyota's production smoothing in Bangalore plant |
| **P4 – Deliver** | Multi-modal transport (road + rail) via finished-car logistics | Using railway rakes (BCN wagons) to dispatch from Manesar to Mumbai |
| **P5 – Return** | Recall management for defective batches | Volkswagen's airbag recall in 2016 (global) |
| **P6 – Enable** | SCOR governance committee with quarterly scorecards | Ford's Global SCOR Steering Council in Detroit |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**
> Students often **confuse Level 3 and Level 4** of SCOR. Level 3 is *defined* by SCOR (e.g., "Schedule Production Activities"), whereas Level 4 is *company-specific* (e.g., "Update SAP Production Order"). Writing the wrong definition costs **2 marks**.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) – Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam – June 2023]

**(a)** Discuss the **GSCF (Global Supply Chain Forum) Model** by Lambert. Compare it with the **SCOR model** along any **five dimensions**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An automotive component manufacturer has recorded the following data for one quarter:
- Perfect orders = 8,200; Total orders = 10,000
- Order Fulfillment Cycle Time = 6 days
- $DIO = 35$, $DSO = 50$, $DPO = 28$ days

**Calculate:**
- (i) Perfect Order Fulfillment (POF) **(3 marks)**
- (ii) Cash-to-Cash Cycle Time (C2C) **(2 marks)**
- (iii) Comment on whether the manufacturer's supply chain performance is **better than or worse than** the industry benchmark. **(2 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for (a):

The **GSCF Model** was developed by Douglas M. Lambert and identifies **8 key cross-functional business processes** that link supply chain members into an integrated network. The processes are grouped under **3 macro-processes**:
- **Customer-focused:** CRM, CSM, DM
- **Internal:** OF, MFM, PDC
- **Supplier-focused:** SRM, PS

**Comparison Table – GSCF vs SCOR:**

| Dimension | SCOR Model | GSCF Model |
|-----------|------------|------------|
| **Origin** | Supply Chain Council (1996) | Global Supply Chain Forum (1998) |
| **Number of Processes** | 6 (P1–P6) | 8 processes across 3 macro-groups |
| **Performance Metrics** | Built-in (Level 1 metrics) | Not pre-defined |
| **Orientation** | Process + Metrics | Relationship + Strategy |
| **Reverse Logistics** | Explicit (P5) | Implicit |
| **Best Fit** | Operational benchmarking | Strategic partnership building |

---

#### Model Solution for (b):

**(i) POF Calculation:**

$$
POF = \frac{\text{Perfect Orders}}{\text{Total Orders}} \times 100\%
$$

$$
POF = \frac{8200}{10000} \times 100\% = 82\%
$$

**(ii) C2C Calculation:**

$$
C2C = DIO + DSO - DPO
$$

$$
C2C = 35 + 50 - 28 = 57 \text{ days}
$$

**(iii) Benchmark Comparison:**

- **POF = 82%** is **at the lower end** of the automotive industry benchmark (typically **85–95%** for world-class OEMs). The manufacturer's reliability needs improvement.
- **C2C = 57 days** is **worse** than the world-class benchmark of **40 days**, indicating the company has too much capital locked in inventory and receivables.

> **Conclusion:** The manufacturer's supply chain is **functional but below world-class**. It must reduce $DSO$ (improve collections) and $DIO$ (improve inventory turnover) by adopting **Lean and SCOR Level-4 practices**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**
> - In POF, students often **forget to multiply by 100%** – you will lose 1 mark.
> - In C2C, students sometimes add DPO instead of subtracting it. Remember: **C2C = DIO + DSO − DPO**.
> - For the comparison sub-part, simply stating "low" or "high" without a benchmark figure will not earn the full 2 marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **SCOR** = **S**upply **C**hain **O**perations **R**eference Model – the most widely used cross-industry SCM framework.
- **Six core processes:** **P1 Plan, P2 Source, P3 Make, P4 Deliver, P5 Return, P6 Enable**.
- **Four hierarchical levels:** Strategic (P1–P6) → Tactical (Categories) → Operational (Elements) → Implementation (Company-specific).
- **Level 4 is NOT defined by SCOR** – it is left to the implementing firm.
- **Five performance attributes (RRACA):** Reliability, Responsiveness, Agility, Costs, Asset Management.
- **Perfect Order Fulfillment (POF)** = (Perfect Orders / Total Orders) × 100%.
- **Cash-to-Cash Cycle Time (C2C)** = $DIO + DSO - DPO$.
- **GSCF Model** has **8 processes** in **3 macro-groups**; developed by Douglas Lambert.
- **Cox's Model** has only **4 processes** (Plan, Source, Make, Deliver).
- **Automotive Industry Adoption:** Toyota (JIT, Lean), Tesla (Agile, Digital), Volkswagen (SCOR, Reverse Logistics), Maruti Suzuki (Hybrid Lean-SC).
- **World-class benchmarks:** POF > 95%, C2C < 40 days.
- **Maturity Stages of SCOR:** Siloed → Functional → Cross-Functional → Aligned → Agile/Networked.
- **Remember the order P1 → P2 → P3 → P4 → P5**, with **P6 (Enable)** running **horizontally** across all.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Role of SCM in the Automotive Industry: Strategic Importance in Reducing Costs, Improving Efficiency, and Enhancing Customer Satisfaction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT753 - SUPPLY CHAIN MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Role of SCM in the Automotive Industry: Strategic Importance in Reducing Costs, Improving Efficiency, and Enhancing Customer Satisfaction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Supply Chain Management (SCM)** in the automotive industry refers to the integrated, end-to-end coordination and strategic orchestration of all activities, entities, information flows, and resources involved in the movement and transformation of raw materials into finished vehicles — from tier-3 sub-suppliers of basic commodities (steel, aluminium, plastics, rare-earth magnets, semiconductors) all the way through tier-1 system integrators, OEM assembly plants, distribution networks, dealerships, and ultimately to the end customer, with the objective of simultaneously maximizing customer value and minimizing systemic costs.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition Alignment:**
> Per the KTU PEAUT753 Module 1 framework, SCM is defined as the *systematic design, planning, execution, control, and monitoring of supply chain activities* with the goal of creating net value, building a competitive infrastructure, leveraging worldwide logistics, synchronizing supply with demand, and measuring performance globally. In the automotive context, this definition extends across **multi-tier supplier networks** spanning **30,000+ parts per vehicle**.

### 1.2 Core Strategic Objectives (The Three Pillars)

The strategic role of SCM in the automotive sector is built on three interconnected pillars:

| **Pillar** | **Strategic Meaning** | **Typical KPI** |
|---|---|---|
| **Cost Reduction** | Lower total landed cost of every vehicle component | Cost per vehicle (CPV), logistics as % of sales |
| **Efficiency Improvement** | Faster cycle times, lean inventory, zero defects | Inventory turnover, Order-to-Delivery (OTD) days |
| **Customer Satisfaction** | Right vehicle, right place, right time, right condition | OTIF rate, CSAT, NPS score |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Orchestra Without a Conductor"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intuition Box — Why SCM is Like a Symphony Orchestra:**
> Imagine an automotive company without SCM as **1,200 musicians scattered across 40 countries, each playing their own tune at their own tempo** — the result is chaos, not a symphony. **SCM is the conductor.** It synchronizes:
> - The **steel mills** (strings section — slow, foundational),
> - The **chip fabs in Taiwan** (woodwinds — precise, high-pitched),
> - The **assembly robots in Pune/Chennai** (brass — powerful, rhythmic),
> - The **logistics trucks and rail networks** (percussion — driving the beat),
> - The **dealerships** (audience-facing crescendo).
>
> Without the conductor (SCM strategy), even the finest musicians (suppliers) produce noise. **With SCM, they produce a Mercedes, a Tata Nexon, or a Toyota Fortuner.**

### 1.4 Standard Industry Metrics Used in Automotive SCM

- **OTIF (On-Time-In-Full):** Industry benchmark target = **≥ 95%**
- **Inventory Turnover Ratio:** Best-in-class = **> 12 times/year**
- **Cash-to-Cash Cycle Time:** World-class OEM = **negative** (e.g., Toyota)
- **Days of Supply (DOS):** Lean target = **< 15 days**
- **Supplier Defect Rate (PPM):** World-class = **< 10 PPM** (Parts Per Million)

> [!NOTE]
> The term **PPM (Parts Per Million)** is the universal automotive quality metric. **1 PPM = 0.0001%** defect rate. If a Tier-1 supplier ships **10 PPM**, it means only 10 defective parts per million components shipped.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Pillar 1 — Strategic Role in Cost Reduction

Automotive manufacturing is a **low-margin, high-volume business**. Profit margins typically range between **5% – 9%** for mass-market OEMs. Therefore, a **1% reduction in supply chain cost** directly translates to a **2% – 3% increase in operating profit** (a concept borrowed from profit-leverage analysis).

#### 2.1.1 The Bulleted Logic of Cost Reduction via SCM

1. **Bulk Procurement Leverage:** Centralized purchasing across global plants (e.g., Maruti Suzuki buying steel for Maruti + Suzuki India + Toyota Kirloskar joint ventures) reduces unit cost by **8% – 15%**.
2. **Supplier Consolidation:** Reducing supplier base from 2,000 to 800 (typical Japanese OEM strategy) cuts transaction, audit, and qualification costs.
3. **Logistics Network Optimization:** Hub-and-spoke warehousing reduces freight cost by **12% – 18%**.
4. **Inventory Carrying Cost Reduction:** Just-In-Time (JIT) and Just-In-Sequence (JIS) delivery reduce warehousing cost by **20% – 30%**.
5. **Reverse Logistics Recovery:** Re-manufacturing of warranty parts recovers **40% – 60%** of original material cost.

### 2.2 Pillar 2 — Strategic Role in Improving Efficiency

Efficiency in automotive SCM is measured along **time, resource utilization, and waste elimination** dimensions, classically inspired by the **Toyota Production System (TPS)** and **Lean Manufacturing** principles.

#### 2.2.1 Efficiency Levers

- **Lead Time Compression:** Reducing component lead time from 45 days to 15 days through digital supplier integration (EDI / API-based ordering).
- **Capacity Utilization:** Synchronizing multi-tier production schedules to keep assembly lines running at **>90% OEE** (Overall Equipment Effectiveness).
- **Waste Elimination (Muda):** Eliminating 7 wastes — defects, overproduction, waiting, transport, inventory, motion, over-processing.
- **Digital Twin & AI Forecasting:** Machine learning demand forecasting reduces bullwhip effect by **30% – 50%**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Bullwhip Effect (Forrester Effect):** A small fluctuation in retail demand causes progressively larger fluctuations upstream at the wholesale, distributor, manufacturer, and raw material supplier levels. In automotive, a **10% demand increase at dealerships** can translate to a **40% order spike at Tier-1 suppliers** if SCM coordination is poor. SCM minimizes this through **demand visibility and information sharing**.

### 2.3 Pillar 3 — Strategic Role in Enhancing Customer Satisfaction

Customer satisfaction in the automotive industry is multi-dimensional:

1. **Product Availability:** Customer finds the desired variant (petrol/diesel, color, trim) in stock.
2. **Delivery Speed:** Order-to-delivery time of **< 14 days** is industry best practice.
3. **Build Quality:** Defect-free delivery (zero-defect targets).
4. **After-Sales Service:** Spare parts availability within **24 hours** for fast-moving parts.
5. **Personalization:** Mass-customization (e.g., Tata, Hyundai offering 100+ variants per model).

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Metric** | **Formula** | **Unit** | **Target Benchmark** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Inventory Turnover (IT)** | $IT = \frac{\text{Cost of Goods Sold (COGS)}}{\text{Average Inventory}}$ | times/year | $> 12$ |
| **Days of Supply (DOS)** | $DOS = \frac{\text{Average Inventory}}{\text{COGS per day}}$ | days | $< 15$ |
| **OTIF Rate** | $\text{OTIF} = \frac{\text{Orders delivered on-time AND in-full}}{\text{Total orders}} \times 100$ | % | $\geq 95\%$ |
| **Supplier Defect Rate (PPM)** | $\text{PPM} = \frac{\text{Defective units}}{\text{Total units received}} \times 1{,}000{,}000$ | PPM | $< 10$ |
| **Cash-to-Cash Cycle Time (C2C)** | $C2C = DIO + DSO - DPO$ | days | Negative (Toyota) |
| **Fill Rate (FR)** | $FR = \frac{\text{Units shipped from stock}}{\text{Total units demanded}} \times 100$ | % | $\geq 98\%$ |
| **Order-to-Delivery (OTD)** | $OTD = \text{Order Date} - \text{Delivery Date}$ | days | $\leq 14$ |
| **Perfect Order Rate (POR)** | $POR = OTIF \times QFR \times ADR \times CDR$ | % | $\geq 90\%$ |
| **Logistics Cost % of Sales** | $LC\% = \frac{\text{Total Logistics Cost}}{\text{Net Sales}} \times 100$ | % | $5\% - 8\%$ |
| **Customer Satisfaction Score (CSAT)** | $\text{CSAT} = \frac{\text{Satisfied responses (4-5/5)}}{\text{Total responses}} \times 100$ | % | $\geq 85\%$ |

Where:
- **DIO** = Days Inventory Outstanding
- **DSO** = Days Sales Outstanding
- **DPO** = Days Payable Outstanding
- **QFR** = Quality Fill Rate
- **ADR** = Accuracy Delivery Rate
- **CDR** = Complete Documentation Rate

### 2.5 Real-World Utility of These Metrics

- **Volkswagen Group** uses IT > 10 as a group-wide lean benchmark.
- **Toyota** operates with **negative C2C cycle** by collecting customer payments before paying suppliers — a financial SCM masterstroke.
- **Tesla** revolutionized automotive SCM with **vertical integration** (manufacturing own batteries at Gigafactories) reducing battery cost per kWh by **56%** between 2016 and 2023.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: The Profit Leverage Effect of SCM Cost Reduction

**Statement:** *Why is a 1% reduction in supply chain cost more valuable than a 1% increase in sales?*

#### **Step 1 — Establish the baseline profit equation**

$$
\text{Operating Profit} = \text{Revenue} - \text{Cost of Goods Sold (COGS)} - \text{Operating Expenses}
$$

#### **Step 2 — Express SC cost as a component of COGS**

In the automotive industry, **60% – 75%** of a vehicle's cost is supply-chain related (raw materials, components, inbound logistics, inventory carrying).

$$
\text{SC Cost} = 0.70 \times \text{COGS}
$$

#### **Step 3 — Compute the impact of a 1% SC cost reduction on profit**

$$
\Delta \text{Profit}_{\text{SC}} = 0.70 \times 0.01 \times \text{COGS}
$$

#### **Step 4 — Compute the impact of a 1% revenue increase on profit**

After tax and variable cost adjustment, only about **8% – 12%** of incremental revenue drops to operating profit:

$$
\Delta \text{Profit}_{\text{Rev}} = 0.10 \times 0.01 \times \text{Revenue}
$$

#### **Step 5 — Establish the ratio (Profit Leverage Factor)**

Assuming COGS ≈ 75% of Revenue:

$$
\frac{\Delta \text{Profit}_{\text{SC}}}{\Delta \text{Profit}_{\text{Rev}}} = \frac{0.70 \times 0.01 \times 0.75 \times \text{Revenue}}{0.10 \times 0.01 \times \text{Revenue}}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\Delta \text{Profit}_{\text{SC}}}{\Delta \text{Profit}_{\text{Rev}}} &= \frac{0.70 \times 0.75}{0.10} \times \frac{\text{Cancel } (0.01 \times \text{Revenue})}{\text{Cancel } (0.01 \times \text{Revenue})} \\
&= \frac{0.5250}{0.1000} \\
&= 5.25
\end{aligned}
$$

#### **Step 6 — Interpret the result**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Inference:** **A 1% reduction in supply chain cost delivers the same profit impact as a 5.25% increase in sales.** This is the *Supply Chain Profit Leverage Effect* — the mathematical foundation of why SCM is a boardroom priority in the automotive industry.

---

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example: Inventory Turnover Calculation

**Problem:** Maruti Suzuki India reported the following FY data:
- COGS = ₹85,000 Crore
- Opening Inventory = ₹5,200 Crore
- Closing Inventory = ₹4,800 Crore

**Find:** (a) Inventory Turnover Ratio, (b) Days of Supply

#### **Step 1 — Compute Average Inventory**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Average Inventory} &= \frac{\text{Opening Inventory} + \text{Closing Inventory}}{2} \\
&= \frac{5{,}200 + 4{,}800}{2} \\
&= \frac{10{,}000}{2} \\
&= 5{,}000 \text{ Crore}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### **Step 2 — Compute Inventory Turnover (IT)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
IT &= \frac{\text{COGS}}{\text{Average Inventory}} \\
&= \frac{85{,}000}{5{,}000} \\
&= 17 \text{ times/year}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### **Step 3 — Compute Days of Supply (DOS)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
DOS &= \frac{\text{Average Inventory}}{\text{COGS per day}} \\
&= \frac{5{,}000}{\frac{85{,}000}{365}} \\
&= \frac{5{,}000 \times 365}{85{,}000} \\
&= \frac{1{,}825{,}000}{85{,}000} \\
&= 21.47 \text{ days}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### **Step 4 — Interpretation**

> [!NOTE]
> **Result Interpretation:** Maruti's IT of **17x** is **above the world-class benchmark of 12x**, and DOS of **~21 days** is acceptable but higher than the **< 15 days** lean target. This indicates room for improvement in inventory optimization — a classic SCM intervention opportunity.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Cause-Effect Linkages in Automotive SCM

| **Strategic Lever** | **SC Activity** | **Cost Impact** | **Efficiency Impact** | **Customer Impact** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Vendor Consolidation** | Reduce supplier base by 40% | ↓ 8% material cost | ↑ 25% faster PO cycles | ↑ 15% quality consistency |
| **JIT Delivery** | Daily/hourly part delivery | ↓ 20% carrying cost | ↑ 30% throughput | ↓ 40% waiting time |
| **Digital Forecasting** | ML-based demand sensing | ↓ 12% obsolescence | ↓ 50% bullwhip | ↑ 25% in-stock rate |
| **Reverse Logistics** | Reclaim end-of-life parts | ↓ 45% material cost | ↑ circular revenue | ↑ sustainability perception |
| **Supplier Co-Location** | Tier-1 near OEM plant | ↓ 18% transport cost | ↓ 60% lead time | ↑ OTIF to 98% |

---

### 3.4 Derivation: Perfect Order Rate Decomposition

**The Perfect Order Rate (POR)** is the gold-standard metric for customer satisfaction in SCM. A "perfect order" must satisfy all four conditions:

$$
POR = OTIF \times QFR \times ADR \times CDR
$$

#### **Numerical Illustration:**
Suppose an OEM reports quarterly:
- OTIF = 96%
- QFR (Quality Fill Rate) = 99%
- ADR (Accurate Documentation Rate) = 98%
- CDR (Complete Documentation Rate) = 97%

$$
\begin{aligned}
POR &= 0.96 \times 0.99 \times 0.98 \times 0.97 \\
&= 0.96 \times 0.99 \times 0.9506 \\
&= 0.96 \times 0.9411 \\
&= 0.9035 \\
&\approx 90.35\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** Even with each component > 96%, the multiplicative compounding drags the perfect order rate to just **90.35%**. This demonstrates the **multiplicative sensitivity** of customer satisfaction — a small weakness in any one SC dimension cascades into significant customer experience degradation. **This is why integrated SCM is non-negotiable in automotive.**

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Model Question]**
**"Define Supply Chain Management and explain its three strategic objectives in the context of the automotive industry."**

**Model Answer:**

Supply Chain Management (SCM) is the integrated planning, execution, and control of all activities involved in the flow of materials, information, and finances from raw material suppliers to the end customer. In the automotive industry, SCM is strategically critical because a single vehicle contains **30,000+ parts** sourced from **1,500+ tier-1 suppliers** across multiple geographies.

The three strategic objectives are:

1. **Cost Reduction:** Lowering the total landed cost of every component through bulk procurement, supplier consolidation, and JIT logistics, thereby improving operating margins in the low-margin auto sector.
2. **Efficiency Improvement:** Reducing lead times, eliminating waste (muda), and synchronizing multi-tier production schedules to maximize throughput and OEE.
3. **Customer Satisfaction:** Ensuring product availability, faster delivery, zero-defect quality, and mass-customization to enhance customer experience and brand loyalty.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Model Question]**
**"What is the Bullwhip Effect? How does effective SCM mitigate it in the automotive industry?"**

**Model Answer:**

The **Bullwhip Effect** (also called the Forrester Effect) is the phenomenon where small fluctuations in end-customer demand cause progressively amplified fluctuations upstream in the supply chain — at dealer, distributor, OEM, and supplier levels. In the automotive industry, a **10% demand surge at dealerships** can trigger a **40% – 70% order spike at Tier-2 raw material suppliers**, leading to overstocking, obsolescence, and excess inventory cost.

Effective SCM mitigates the Bullwhip Effect through:
1. **Information Sharing:** Real-time POS data from dealers to OEMs via EDI/cloud platforms.
2. **Demand Forecasting:** AI/ML-based collaborative forecasting between OEMs and Tier-1 suppliers.
3. **Vendor-Managed Inventory (VMI):** Suppliers monitor and replenish stock autonomously.
4. **Order Stabilization:** Smaller, more frequent orders rather than large batch purchases.
5. **Lead Time Reduction:** Compressing replenishment lead time dampens the amplification.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Pattern]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain in detail the strategic role of Supply Chain Management in **reducing costs** in the automotive industry. Support your answer with the concept of the *Supply Chain Profit Leverage Effect* and at least four cost-reduction levers.

**(b) [7 Marks]** With a suitable numerical illustration, compute the **Inventory Turnover Ratio** and **Days of Supply (DOS)** for an automotive OEM given COGS = ₹72,000 Cr, Opening Inventory = ₹6,000 Cr, Closing Inventory = ₹4,000 Cr. Comment on whether the OEM meets the world-class lean benchmark.

---

### **Model Solution — Question A:**

**(a) [7 Marks] Strategic Role of SCM in Cost Reduction**

The automotive industry operates on thin operating margins of **5% – 9%**, making every cost rupee strategically vital. SCM contributes to cost reduction through four primary levers:

**Lever 1 — Centralized Bulk Procurement:**
By aggregating demand across global plants, OEMs negotiate **8% – 15% volume discounts** on steel, aluminium, electronics, and semiconductors. Example: Maruti Suzuki's centralized procurement cell.

**Lever 2 — Supplier Base Optimization (Consolidation):**
Reducing the supplier base from 2,000 to 800 vendors cuts transaction, audit, qualification, and engineering-change costs. Japanese OEMs (Toyota, Honda) follow this lean-sourcing philosophy.

**Lever 3 — Just-In-Time (JIT) and Just-In-Sequence (JIS) Logistics:**
Synchronized inbound delivery eliminates warehouse rent, insurance, and inventory obsolescence costs — typically saving **20% – 30%** of carrying cost.

**Lever 4 — Reverse Logistics and Remanufacturing:**
Recovering warranty cores and end-of-life vehicles for parts remanufacturing recovers **40% – 60%** of original material value, reducing virgin material demand.

**The Supply Chain Profit Leverage Effect:**
Since **60% – 75%** of vehicle cost is supply-chain-related, every **1% reduction in SC cost** yields a profit impact equivalent to a **5% – 6% increase in sales** — making SCM cost reduction the single highest-leverage profit lever available to automotive management.

**(b) [7 Marks] Numerical Solution**

**Given:**
- COGS = ₹72,000 Crore
- Opening Inventory = ₹6,000 Crore
- Closing Inventory = ₹4,000 Crore

**Step 1 — Average Inventory:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Avg Inventory} &= \frac{6{,}000 + 4{,}000}{2} \\
&= \frac{10{,}000}{2} \\
&= 5{,}000 \text{ Crore}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Inventory Turnover (IT):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
IT &= \frac{\text{COGS}}{\text{Avg Inventory}} \\
&= \frac{72{,}000}{5{,}000} \\
&= 14.4 \text{ times/year}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Days of Supply (DOS):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
DOS &= \frac{\text{Avg Inventory}}{\text{COGS per day}} \\
&= \frac{5{,}000}{\frac{72{,}000}{365}} \\
&= \frac{5{,}000 \times 365}{72{,}000} \\
&= \frac{1{,}825{,}000}{72{,}000} \\
&= 25.35 \text{ days}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Benchmark Comment:**
- The IT of **14.4x** is **above the world-class benchmark of 12x** → **strong performance**.
- The DOS of **~25 days** exceeds the lean target of **< 15 days** → **improvement required**.
- *Inference:* The OEM has good turnover velocity but should pursue further inventory reduction through JIT and demand-driven MRP (DDMRP) to reach lean benchmarks.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam — Model Alternate]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss the role of SCM in **improving operational efficiency** in the automotive industry. Explain the concepts of **Lean Manufacturing, Just-In-Time, and the Bullwhip Effect** with relevant examples.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain in detail the role of SCM in **enhancing customer satisfaction**. Compute the **Perfect Order Rate (POR)** given OTIF = 95%, QFR = 98%, ADR = 97%, CDR = 96%, and interpret the result.

---

### **Model Solution — Question B:**

**(a) [7 Marks] Role of SCM in Efficiency Improvement**

**Concept 1 — Lean Manufacturing:**
Originating from the Toyota Production System (TPS), lean manufacturing targets the elimination of seven wastes (*muda*): defects, overproduction, waiting, transportation, inventory, motion, and over-processing. SCM coordinates supplier deliveries to feed lean assembly lines without buffers.

**Concept 2 — Just-In-Time (JIT):**
JIT ensures that the right parts arrive at the right workstation exactly when needed — eliminating WIP inventory. For example, Tata Motors' Pune plant uses JIT sequencing for engine and transmission subassemblies, reducing dock-to-line time to under 4 hours.

**Concept 3 — Bullwhip Effect Mitigation:**
The Bullwhip Effect causes demand-signal distortion upstream. SCM counters this through:
- **CPFR (Collaborative Planning, Forecasting, and Replenishment)** between OEMs and suppliers.
- **Digital twin simulations** to model demand scenarios.
- **Vendor-Managed Inventory (VMI)** where the supplier (not the OEM) decides replenishment.

**Result:** Lower safety stocks, faster OTD, and higher throughput.

**(b) [7 Marks] Role of SCM in Customer Satisfaction + POR Computation**

**Strategic Levers for Customer Satisfaction:**
1. **Product Availability:** Right SKU in stock at the right dealer (Fill Rate ≥ 98%).
2. **Delivery Speed:** OTD ≤ 14 days.
3. **Build Quality:** Zero-defect target (< 10 PPM).
4. **After-Sales Service:** Spare parts availability within 24 hours.
5. **Mass Customization:** Hyundai, Kia, and Tata offer 100+ variant combinations per model.

**POR Computation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
POR &= OTIF \times QFR \times ADR \times CDR \\
&= 0.95 \times 0.98 \times 0.97 \times 0.96
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step-by-step:**
- $0.95 \times 0.98 = 0.9310$
- $0.9310 \times 0.97 = 0.90307$
- $0.90307 \times 0.96 = 0.86695$

$$
POR \approx 86.70\%
$$

**Interpretation:**
Although each component exceeds 95%, the multiplicative compounding reduces the POR to **86.70%**, falling short of the world-class benchmark of **≥ 90%**. To improve, the OEM must simultaneously raise all four dimensions — highlighting that **customer satisfaction is a multiplicative, not additive, function of SCM performance**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing "Inventory Turnover" with "Inventory Days"** — these are reciprocals; students often write the wrong unit. (1 mark lost frequently)
> 2. **Ignoring the multiplicative nature of POR** — students treat it additively and lose 2–3 marks.
> 3. **Defining JIT without an example** — KTU examiners deduct marks for textbook-only definitions; always cite a real OEM example (Tata, Maruti, Toyota).
> 4. **Skipping the "Comment/Interpretation" step** in numerical problems — KTU's 2024 scheme explicitly tests **analytical interpretation** as a separate CO under Bloom's "Evaluate" level.
> 5. **Forgetting to convert COGS to daily terms** when computing DOS — a 2-mark deduction trap.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Module 1 / Automotive SCM:**
>
> - **SCM Definition:** End-to-end coordination of materials, information, and finance from raw material to end customer.
> - **Automotive SC Complexity:** **30,000+ parts per vehicle**, **1,500+ tier-1 suppliers**, multi-tier global network.
> - **Three Strategic Pillars:** Cost Reduction, Efficiency Improvement, Customer Satisfaction.
> - **Profit Leverage Effect:** **1% SC cost reduction ≈ 5% sales increase** in profit impact.
> - **Bullwhip Effect:** Demand variability amplifies upstream; mitigated by CPFR, VMI, and information sharing.
> - **Key Metrics to Memorize:** OTIF (≥95%), PPM (<10), IT (>12), DOS (<15), POR (≥90%), C2C (negative for Toyota).
> - **Lean Pillars:** JIT, JIS, VMI, CPFR, TQM, Six Sigma, Kaizen.
> - **POR is multiplicative,** not additive — even small weaknesses cascade.
> - **Toyota Production System (TPS)** is the foundational reference model for lean automotive SCM.
> - **Reverse Logistics** recovers **40% – 60%** of material cost and supports circular economy.
> - **Digital Transformation:** AI forecasting, IoT-enabled tracking, digital twins, and blockchain-based supplier traceability are the **future of automotive SCM**.
> - **KTU Bloom's Level Tested:** Remember (define), Understand (explain), Apply (compute IT/DOS), Analyze (interpret POR), Evaluate (compare OEMs).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Long-Term Planning and Decision-Making in Supply Chain Management
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT753 - SUPPLY CHAIN MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Long-Term Planning and Decision-Making in Supply Chain Management

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Long-Term Planning and Decision-Making in Supply Chain Management (SCM)** refers to the **strategic-level decision horizon** (typically spanning **3 to 10 years**) in which an organization establishes its overarching supply chain configuration, including the structure of its distribution networks, the location and capacity of facilities, the sourcing strategy for products, and the design of information and transportation flows. These decisions are **irreversible or very costly to reverse**, and they dictate the long-run competitive position of the firm in its industry.

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme PEAUT753 syllabus**, this topic encompasses three classical pillars:

1. **Supply Chain Strategy** — defining the supply chain's role as a competitive weapon.
2. **Strategic Decision Phases** — distinguishing strategic, tactical, and operational decisions.
3. **Strategic Sourcing & Make-or-Buy Decisions** — determining which value-adding activities the firm should perform internally versus those that should be outsourced.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examiner's Note:** Long-term (strategic) decisions in SCM are characterized by **high capital commitment**, **long impact horizon**, and **low frequency of revision**. They sit at the **top of the decision hierarchy** in the classical Chopra-Meindl framework.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine building a **dream house**:
- The **number of floors, location of the house, and number of rooms** = **Long-term (strategic) decisions**.
- The **furniture placement, paint colors, monthly electricity budget** = **Tactical decisions**.
- The **daily schedule for housekeeping and grocery shopping** = **Operational decisions**.

Once the house is built, you cannot easily relocate its foundation or change the number of floors — this is exactly the nature of **long-term supply chain decisions**. Choosing the wrong location for a distribution center or a manufacturing plant can lock the firm into decades of inefficient logistics costs.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** The KTU 2024 PEAUT753 syllabus explicitly clusters this topic under Module 2 with a focus on **strategic decisions**, **decision hierarchies**, and **make-or-buy analysis**.

### 1.3 Key Terminology

| Term | Definition |
|---|---|
| **Strategic Horizon** | Long-term planning window (3–10 years). |
| **Tactical Horizon** | Medium-term planning window (quarters to a year). |
| **Operational Horizon** | Short-term planning window (daily to weekly). |
| **Decision Phases** | Stratification of decisions by time horizon and impact. |
| **Make-or-Buy** | Decision whether to produce in-house or outsource. |
| **Strategic Sourcing** | Long-term procurement planning aligned with supply chain strategy. |
| **SCOR Model** | Supply Chain Operations Reference model by APICS. |

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three-Phase Decision Framework in SCM

Supply chain decisions are stratified into three hierarchical phases based on the **frequency of execution** and the **time horizon of impact**. This is the foundational classification used in the seminal text by **Chopra & Meindl** and adopted in KTU's 2024 scheme.

#### Phase 1 — Strategic (Long-Term) Decisions
- **Time Horizon:** Typically **3 to 10 years**.
- **Frequency:** Once every few years (rarely revisited).
- **Cost of Reversal:** **Very high** — almost irreversible.
- **Examples:**
  * Number, location, and capacity of **manufacturing plants** and **distribution centers**.
  * Choice of **transportation modes** (air, sea, rail, road) as primary infrastructure.
  * **Make-or-buy** decisions for components.
  * Selection of **suppliers** for long-term strategic partnerships.
  * **Supply chain configuration** (push vs. pull boundary, centralization vs. decentralization).
  * **Information systems** and IT infrastructure investment.

#### Phase 2 — Tactical (Medium-Term) Decisions
- **Time Horizon:** **Quarterly to annual** basis.
- **Frequency:** Monthly or quarterly revisions.
- **Cost of Reversal:** Moderate.
- **Examples:**
  * Inventory policy decisions (safety stock levels, reorder points).
  * Production planning and master production schedules.
  * Transportation consolidation and routing decisions.
  * Supplier performance evaluation and contract renegotiation.
  * Workforce sizing and shift scheduling.

#### Phase 3 — Operational (Short-Term) Decisions
- **Time Horizon:** **Daily to weekly**.
- **Frequency:** Real-time or hourly.
- **Cost of Reversal:** Low.
- **Examples:**
  * Order fulfillment and dispatch.
  * Daily production sequencing.
  * Truck loading and last-mile delivery scheduling.
  * Real-time demand allocation.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why This Matters:** The KTU board examiner often frames a 14-mark question around the **three-phase framework**, asking students to **classify** and **contrast** decisions across phases. Mastering the time-horizon × impact × reversibility triangle is essential.

### 2.2 Supply Chain Strategy

A **supply chain strategy** defines the long-term approach the firm will use to integrate and coordinate its supply chain activities to gain competitive advantage. The **Chopra-Meindl strategic framework** identifies that the supply chain strategy must be aligned with:

1. **The company's competitive strategy** (cost leadership, differentiation, responsiveness).
2. **The customer's needs** (product variety, lead time, price, availability).
3. **The competitive environment** (industry structure, barriers to entry).

#### The Four Strategic Subsystems
1. **Product development strategy** — long-term R&D and product introduction plans.
2. **Procurement and sourcing strategy** — make-or-buy, supplier selection.
3. **Distribution and logistics strategy** — centralized vs. decentralized networks.
4. **Inventory and information strategy** — buffer placement, push/pull interface.

### 2.3 Make-or-Buy Decision

The **make-or-buy decision** is a classic long-term strategic choice that determines whether a particular component, service, or process should be produced internally (make) or acquired from an external supplier (buy).

#### Reasons to MAKE (Insource)
- Protect proprietary technology / trade secrets.
- Ensure quality control and tight specifications.
- Avoid dependency on suppliers for critical components.
- Utilize existing excess capacity.
- Protect long-term strategic capabilities.

#### Reasons to BUY (Outsource)
- Free up internal capacity for core competencies.
- Gain access to specialized expertise.
- Reduce capital investment risk.
- Improve flexibility to demand fluctuations.
- Lower total cost of ownership.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Concept | Mathematical Expression | Description | Units / Domain |
|---|---|---|---|
| Total Cost of Make | $TC_{\text{make}} = C_{\text{prod}} + C_{\text{fixed}} + C_{\text{inv}}$ | Internal production cost | Currency / period |
| Total Cost of Buy | $TC_{\text{buy}} = P_{\text{purchase}} + C_{\text{log}} + C_{\text{qual}}$ | External purchase cost | Currency / period |
| Break-Even Volume | $V^{*} = \dfrac{F}{p - v}$ | Volume where make = buy cost | Units / year |
| Strategic Horizon | $H_{\text{strat}} \in [3, 10]$ years | Time window of long-term decisions | Years |
| Decision Frequency Ratio | $f_{\text{strat}} \ll f_{\text{tac}} \ll f_{\text{op}}$ | Frequency hierarchy | Dimensionless |
| Strategic Fit Index | $\text{SFI} = \alpha C_{\text{strat}} + \beta S_{\text{strat}}$ | Alignment of capabilities and strategy | Index value |
| Total Logistics Cost | $TLC = C_{\text{transport}} + C_{\text{inventory}} + C_{\text{handling}}$ | Aggregate network cost | Currency |
| Inventory Turnover | $IT = \dfrac{\text{COGS}}{\text{Avg. Inventory}}$ | Operational efficiency metric | Times / year |
| Pipeline Inventory | $I_{\text{pipe}} = D \cdot L$ | Inventory in transit | Units |
| Safety Stock | $SS = z \cdot \sigma_{LT} \cdot \sqrt{L}$ | Buffer against variability | Units |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examination Tip:** In all formulas, never confuse **strategic cost of reversal** with **tactical cost of reversal**. The mark scheme deducts points when students incorrectly interchange the impact horizons.

### 2.5 Real-World Utility

Long-term SCM planning is the foundation of **global competitive advantage**:
- **Amazon's** distributed warehouse network was a 20-year strategic commitment.
- **Apple's** make-or-buy strategy for chip fabrication (now pivoting in-house via TSMC partnerships) is a strategic-level decision.
- **Toyota's** lean supply chain strategy integrates tactical inventory policies (JIT) with strategic make-or-buy choices.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Make-or-Buy Break-Even Volume

Let us derive the precise volume of demand at which the **cost of making** a component internally is exactly equal to the **cost of buying** it externally.

#### Step 1: Define Cost Functions

Let:
- $F$ = **Fixed cost** of in-house production (equipment, tooling, setup), in currency/year.
- $v$ = **Variable cost** per unit produced internally, in currency/unit.
- $p$ = **Purchase price** per unit from external supplier, in currency/unit.
- $V$ = **Annual volume** of demand, in units/year.

#### Step 2: Total Cost of MAKE (Internal Production)

The total cost of producing $V$ units internally is the sum of fixed costs and variable costs:

$$TC_{\text{make}}(V) = F + v \cdot V$$

This is a **linear function** with y-intercept $F$ and slope $v$.

#### Step 3: Total Cost of BUY (External Purchase)

The total cost of purchasing $V$ units externally is simply the purchase price times volume (assuming no major fixed cost):

$$TC_{\text{buy}}(V) = p \cdot V$$

This is a **linear function** with y-intercept $0$ and slope $p$.

#### Step 4: Equate Both Costs at Break-Even

To find the break-even volume, set the two cost functions equal:

$$F + v \cdot V^{*} = p \cdot V^{*}$$

#### Step 5: Solve for $V^{*}$

Move all terms involving $V^{*}$ to one side:

$$F = p \cdot V^{*} - v \cdot V^{*}$$

Factor out $V^{*}$:

$$F = V^{*} \cdot (p - v)$$

Therefore:

$$V^{*} = \dfrac{F}{p - v}$$

This is the **break-even volume** — the demand above which it is cheaper to MAKE, and below which it is cheaper to BUY (assuming $p > v$).

#### Step 6: Decision Rule

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{If } V > V^{*} &: \text{ MAKE internally is economically justified} \\
\text{If } V < V^{*} &: \text{ BUY from external supplier is preferable} \\
\text{If } V = V^{*} &: \text{ Indifferent; consider qualitative factors}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Worked Numerical Example

> **Problem:** A firm faces $F = 5{,}00{,}000$ ₹/year in fixed setup costs, $v = 50$ ₹/unit variable cost, and $p = 75$ ₹/unit purchase price. Find the break-even volume and recommend a decision at $V = 12{,}000$ units.

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V^{*} &= \dfrac{F}{p - v} = \dfrac{5{,}00{,}000}{75 - 50} \\
&= \dfrac{5{,}00{,}000}{25} \\
&= 20{,}000 \text{ units/year}
\end{aligned}
$$

Since the actual demand $V = 12{,}000 < V^{*} = 20{,}000$, the **BUY** option is economically preferred, saving:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Savings} &= (F + v \cdot V) - (p \cdot V) \\
&= (5{,}00{,}000 + 50 \cdot 12{,}000) - (75 \cdot 12{,}000) \\
&= (5{,}00{,}000 + 6{,}00{,}000) - 9{,}00{,}000 \\
&= 11{,}00{,}000 - 9{,}00{,}000 \\
&= 2{,}00{,}000 \text{ ₹/year}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.2 Derivation of Strategic Inventory Investment Trade-off

In long-term SCM planning, the firm must balance **facility cost** vs. **inventory holding cost**.

Let:
- $N$ = Number of facilities (distribution centers).
- $F_i$ = Fixed facility cost for facility $i$.
- $H$ = Inventory holding cost per unit per year.
- $D$ = Annual demand.
- $L_N$ = Average lead time when using $N$ facilities.

The **total cost** is:

$$TC(N) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} F_i + H \cdot D \cdot L_N$$

As $N$ increases, $\sum F_i$ rises but $L_N$ (and thus inventory) falls. The optimal $N$ minimizes $TC(N)$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dfrac{d(TC)}{dN} &= 0 \\
\dfrac{d}{dN}\left[\sum_{i=1}^{N} F_i + H \cdot D \cdot L_N\right] &= 0
\end{aligned}
$$

Solving yields the classical result:

$$N^{*} = \sqrt{\dfrac{H \cdot D \cdot L_{\max}}{F}}$$

where $L_{\max}$ is the maximum achievable lead time at $N = 1$.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Decision Classification

| Decision Type | Horizon | Reversibility | Example | Strategic Weight |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Plant location | 5–10 years | Almost none | Setting up a new factory in Pune | **Very High** |
| Distribution center location | 3–7 years | Very low | Adding a regional DC | **Very High** |
| Make-or-buy | 3–5 years | Low | Outsourcing PCB assembly | **High** |
| Supplier selection (long-term) | 3–5 years | Low | 5-year supply contract | **High** |
| Transport mode mix | 3–5 years | Moderate | Investing in dedicated rail fleet | **High** |
| Safety stock policy | 3–12 months | Moderate | Setting $z = 2.05$ | **Medium** |
| Production scheduling | Weekly | High | Daily shift allocation | **Low** |
| Order dispatch | Daily | Very high | Releasing an order | **Very Low** |

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Question 1.** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**"Distinguish between strategic, tactical, and operational decisions in a supply chain. Provide one example of each."**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Supply chain decisions are classified into three hierarchical phases based on **time horizon, frequency, and reversibility**:

1. **Strategic (Long-Term) Decisions** *(1 mark)*
   - **Time horizon:** 3 to 10 years.
   - **Frequency:** Rare, almost irreversible.
   - **Example:** Deciding the **location and capacity of a new manufacturing plant** in India for the next decade.

2. **Tactical (Medium-Term) Decisions** *(1 mark)*
   - **Time horizon:** Quarterly to annual.
   - **Frequency:** Revisions every few months.
   - **Example:** Setting the **safety stock level** for a particular SKU based on quarterly demand forecasts.

3. **Operational (Short-Term) Decisions** *(1 mark)*
   - **Time horizon:** Daily to weekly.
   - **Frequency:** Real-time, fully reversible.
   - **Example:** **Allocating** a specific customer order to a particular distribution center for next-day dispatch.

---

**Question 2.** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**"List and briefly explain any three factors that influence the make-or-buy decision in supply chain management."**

**Model Answer (3 Marks — 1 mark each):**

1. **Capacity Availability:** If the firm has excess in-house capacity, it favors the MAKE decision to utilize idle resources; otherwise, BUY becomes attractive.
2. **Proprietary Technology & IP Protection:** When the component involves patents, trade secrets, or core competitive technology, the firm strongly prefers MAKE to avoid IP leakage.
3. **Total Cost Economics:** The break-even volume $V^{*} = F / (p - v)$ is computed; demand above $V^{*}$ favors MAKE, while demand below $V^{*}$ favors BUY.

*(Acceptable additional factors: supplier reliability, lead time, quality control, demand variability, strategic alliances, and risk diversification.)*

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

`[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]`

**(a)** *Explain in detail the three-phase framework of supply chain decision-making. Discuss the characteristics, time horizon, and typical decisions taken at each phase with suitable examples.* **(7 marks)**

**(b)** *A firm has fixed in-house production cost of ₹6,00,000 per year, variable cost per unit of ₹40, and an external purchase price of ₹70 per unit. Calculate the break-even volume. If the expected demand is 18,000 units per year, advise the firm whether to make or buy. Justify with cost calculations.* **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question A

**(a) Three-Phase Framework of Supply Chain Decision-Making (7 Marks)**

Supply chain decisions are stratified by their **time horizon, frequency, cost of reversal, and strategic impact**. The Chopra-Meindl framework recognizes three distinct phases:

**1. Strategic Decisions (Long-Term) — 3 Marks**

- **Time Horizon:** 3 to 10 years.
- **Frequency of Revision:** Once every few years.
- **Cost of Reversal:** Very high; almost irreversible.
- **Examples:**
  * **Location and capacity** of manufacturing plants and distribution centers.
  * **Make-or-buy** decisions for strategic components.
  * **Selection of long-term suppliers** and outsourcing partners.
  * **Choice of transportation modes** (rail vs. road vs. air) as primary infrastructure.
  * **Design of supply chain network** (centralized vs. decentralized).

**2. Tactical Decisions (Medium-Term) — 2 Marks**

- **Time Horizon:** Quarterly to annual.
- **Frequency of Revision:** Monthly or quarterly.
- **Cost of Reversal:** Moderate.
- **Examples:**
  * **Inventory policies** (safety stock, reorder point).
  * **Master production schedules** for the quarter.
  * **Transportation routing plans** and consolidation strategies.
  * **Workforce sizing** for the next quarter.

**3. Operational Decisions (Short-Term) — 2 Marks**

- **Time Horizon:** Daily to weekly.
- **Frequency of Revision:** Real-time, hourly, or daily.
- **Cost of Reversal:** Very low; highly reversible.
- **Examples:**
  * **Order allocation and dispatch.**
  * **Daily production sequencing** on the shop floor.
  * **Truck loading** and last-mile delivery.
  * **Real-time demand allocation** between facilities.

The three phases are **interdependent**: strategic decisions set the **boundaries** within which tactical and operational decisions are optimized. A poorly chosen plant location (strategic) cannot be fully compensated by excellent routing (tactical) or dispatch (operational).

---

**(b) Break-Even Volume Calculation and Make-or-Buy Recommendation (7 Marks)**

**Given Data:**
- $F = 6{,}00{,}000$ ₹/year (fixed setup cost).
- $v = 40$ ₹/unit (variable cost of internal production).
- $p = 70$ ₹/unit (external purchase price).
- $V = 18{,}000$ units/year (expected demand).

**Step 1: Compute the Break-Even Volume** *(3 marks)*

$$
\begin{aligned}
V^{*} &= \dfrac{F}{p - v} \\
&= \dfrac{6{,}00{,}000}{70 - 40} \\
&= \dfrac{6{,}00{,}000}{30} \\
&= 20{,}000 \text{ units/year}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Compare Actual Demand with Break-Even** *(2 marks)*

Since $V = 18{,}000 < V^{*} = 20{,}000$, the **BUY** option is economically preferable at this demand level.

**Step 3: Compute Cost Savings under BUY** *(2 marks)*

$$
\begin{aligned}
TC_{\text{make}} &= F + v \cdot V = 6{,}00{,}000 + (40 \times 18{,}000) \\
&= 6{,}00{,}000 + 7{,}20{,}000 = 13{,}20{,}000 \text{ ₹}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
TC_{\text{buy}} &= p \cdot V = 70 \times 18{,}000 = 12{,}60{,}000 \text{ ₹}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Savings} &= TC_{\text{make}} - TC_{\text{buy}} \\
&= 13{,}20{,}000 - 12{,}60{,}000 \\
&= 60{,}000 \text{ ₹/year}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Recommendation:** The firm should **BUY** the component from an external supplier, saving ₹60,000 per year, subject to qualitative factors such as supplier reliability and quality.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

`[KTU University Exam — May 2023]`

**(a)** *Explain the concept of supply chain strategy. Discuss the four key strategic decisions (product development, procurement, distribution, and information) and how they align with the company's competitive strategy.* **(7 marks)**

**(b)** *A logistics firm is evaluating whether to establish its own fleet of trucks (MAKE) or hire a third-party logistics provider (BUY). The fixed cost of owning the fleet is ₹15,00,000 per year.
