The variable cost per ton-km is ₹3, and the 3PL charges ₹6 per ton-km. The expected annual throughput is 4,00,000 ton-km. Recommend the optimal decision using break-even analysis.* **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question B

**(a) Supply Chain Strategy (7 Marks)**

**Definition (2 marks):**
A **supply chain strategy** is a long-term plan that defines how the firm will structure and coordinate its supply chain activities — including sourcing, production, distribution, and information flow — to achieve competitive advantage. It must be tightly aligned with the firm's **competitive strategy** (cost leadership, differentiation, or responsiveness) and with **customer needs** (price, variety, lead time, availability).

**The Four Strategic Subsystems (5 marks — 1.25 each):**

1. **Product Development Strategy:**
   Defines how new products will be designed, tested, and introduced. Strategic decisions include R&D investment, design-for-manufacturing, and the timing of product launches. For a **differentiation** strategy, the firm invests heavily in innovation.

2. **Procurement and Sourcing Strategy:**
   Long-term make-or-buy decisions, supplier selection, and the choice between single-sourcing and multi-sourcing. For a **cost leadership** strategy, the firm aggressively pursues low-cost global sourcing.

3. **Distribution and Logistics Strategy:**
   Decisions on the number, location, and capacity of warehouses; choice of transportation modes; and the degree of centralization. For a **responsiveness** strategy, the firm uses decentralized networks close to customers.

4. **Inventory and Information Strategy:**
   Decisions on where to place buffers (raw materials, WIP, or finished goods), how much safety stock to hold, and what IT systems to deploy. For a **cost leadership** strategy, inventory is minimized through JIT and information sharing.

These four strategic subsystems must be **mutually consistent** and **aligned** with the firm's competitive strategy to achieve what Chopra & Meindl term **strategic fit**.

---

**(b) Break-Even Analysis for Fleet vs. 3PL (7 Marks)**

**Given Data:**
- $F = 15{,}00{,}000$ ₹/year.
- $v = 3$ ₹/ton-km (variable cost of own fleet).
- $p = 6$ ₹/ton-km (3PL charge).
- $V = 4{,}00{,}000$ ton-km/year.

**Step 1: Compute Break-Even Throughput** *(3 marks)*

$$
\begin{aligned}
V^{*} &= \dfrac{F}{p - v} \\
&= \dfrac{15{,}00{,}000}{6 - 3} \\
&= \dfrac{15{,}00{,}000}{3} \\
&= 5{,}00{,}000 \text{ ton-km/year}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Compare Actual Throughput with Break-Even** *(2 marks)*

Since $V = 4{,}00{,}000 < V^{*} = 5{,}00{,}000$, the **BUY (3PL)** option is more economical at this throughput.

**Step 3: Compute Cost Differential** *(2 marks)*

$$
\begin{aligned}
TC_{\text{make}} &= F + v \cdot V = 15{,}00{,}000 + (3 \times 4{,}00{,}000) \\
&= 15{,}00{,}000 + 12{,}00{,}000 = 27{,}00{,}000 \text{ ₹}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
TC_{\text{buy}} &= p \cdot V = 6 \times 4{,}00{,}000 = 24{,}00{,}000 \text{ ₹}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Savings} &= 27{,}00{,}000 - 24{,}00{,}000 = 3{,}00{,}000 \text{ ₹/year}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Recommendation:** The firm should **HIRE the 3PL provider**, saving ₹3,00,000 per year, provided the 3PL can meet the required service-level standards. If throughput is expected to exceed 5,00,000 ton-km/year in future, the firm should revisit and consider insourcing.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Typically Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Conflating time horizons:** Students often misclassify tactical decisions (e.g., safety stock) as strategic. *(−1 to 2 marks per question)*
> 2. **Skipping the cost differential computation:** Board examiners expect the explicit calculation of $TC_{\text{make}}$ and $TC_{\text{buy}}$ — not just the break-even formula. *(−2 marks)*
> 3. **Ignoring qualitative factors:** In make-or-buy, KTU expects at least one mention of IP protection, supplier risk, or core competency. *(−1 mark)*
> 4. **Forgetting units:** Writing "20,000" without "units/year" loses 0.5 mark.
> 5. **No strategic-vs-tactical comparison:** In 7-mark theoretical questions, failing to provide a comparison table results in 2-mark deduction.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Long-term planning in SCM** operates over a **3–10 year horizon** with **very high cost of reversal**.
- **Three-phase decision hierarchy:** **Strategic (long-term) → Tactical (medium-term) → Operational (short-term)**.
- **Strategic decisions** include plant location, capacity, make-or-buy, supplier selection, distribution network design, and IT infrastructure.
- **Make-or-buy break-even formula:** $\;V^{*} = F / (p - v)$.
- **MAKE** is preferred when: $V > V^{*}$, IP protection is needed, or capacity is underutilized.
- **BUY** is preferred when: $V < V^{*}$, specialized expertise is needed, or capital risk must be avoided.
- **Four strategic subsystems:** Product development, Procurement, Distribution, and Information strategy — all must achieve **strategic fit** with the competitive strategy.
- **Cost reversal hierarchy:** $C_{\text{reversal}}^{\text{strategic}} \gg C_{\text{reversal}}^{\text{tactical}} \gg C_{\text{reversal}}^{\text{operational}}$.
- **Qualitative factors** (IP, lead time, supplier risk) must complement quantitative break-even analysis.
- **Optimal number of facilities** is found by trading off facility fixed cost against inventory holding cost: $N^{*} = \sqrt{(H \cdot D \cdot L_{\max}) / F}$.
- **Always express final answers with units** — KTU valuation penalizes unit-less numerical answers.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Demand Forecasting and Planning: Techniques for Predicting Customer Demand and Aligning Production Schedules
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEAUT753 - SUPPLY CHAIN MANAGEMENT > - **Module:** Module Module 2 > - **Topic:** Demand Forecasting and Planning: Techniques for Predicting Customer Demand and Aligning Production Schedules ## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Demand Forecasting** is the systematic, scientific process of estimating future customer demand for a product or service over a specified time horizon, using historical data, statistical techniques, market intelligence, and causal models, to enable proactive supply chain decision-making. In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PEAUT753)**, demand forecasting is positioned as the *nervous system of the supply chain*. It is the critical upstream activity that feeds into: - **Production Planning & Scheduling (PPS)** - **Inventory Management (EOQ, Safety Stock)** - **Procurement and Supplier Coordination** - **Distribution Requirements Planning (DRP)** - **Master Production Schedule (MPS) alignment** > [!NOTE] > **Key Terminology (Aligned with KTU Module 2 Outcomes):** > - **Forecast Horizon:** The length of time into the future for which the forecast is prepared (Short-term ≤ 3 months, Medium-term 3–12 months, Long-term > 1 year). > - **Forecasting Accuracy:** Measured typically using **MAD (Mean Absolute Deviation)**, **MAPE (Mean Absolute Percentage Error)**, or **MSE (Mean Squared Error)**. > - **Demand Planning:** The broader business process that translates forecasts into actionable production, inventory, and procurement plans. --- ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview Imagine you run a **chai (tea) stall** outside a KTU engineering college in Kerala. Every day you need to decide **how many cups of chai to prepare** before the morning rush of students arrives. - If you prepare **too much**, the excess chai goes waste (overstocking cost). - If you prepare **too little**, students go to the competitor stall and you lose revenue (stockout cost). So you start **observing patterns**: - On rainy Mondays → fewer students → less chai needed - On exam weeks → students skip class → less chai - On placement season → visiting companies bring crowds → more chai - During Onam/Vishu holidays → campus is empty → very less chai You also track: - Past sales registers (historical data) - Weather forecasts (causal/exogenous variables) - College calendar (event-driven demand) **This entire act of looking at past data, external factors, and projecting future chai requirements IS demand forecasting.** And once you know "I need 200 cups tomorrow," you decide to buy 5 kg tea powder, 20 L milk, etc. — this is **demand planning** translating the forecast into operational action. > [!TIP] > **The Chai Stall Rule:** Forecast = the *guess* of demand. Plan = the *action* to satisfy that guess. No forecast is perfect, but a good one minimizes the total cost of being wrong. --- ### 1.3 Core Components of Demand Forecasting in KTU Context | Component | Description | KTU Relevance | |-----------|-------------|---------------| | **Demand Sensing** | Real-time capture of short-term demand signals | Critical for e-commerce, FMCG | | **Demand Shaping** | Influencing demand via pricing, promotions | Marketing-SCM interface | | **Statistical Forecasting** | Quantitative models (Moving Average, Exponential Smoothing, ARIMA) | Module 2 core topic | | **Collaborative Planning** | CPFR with suppliers and retailers | Advanced Module 3-4 | | **S&OP (Sales & Operations Planning)** | Monthly executive alignment of forecast and supply | Strategic Module 4 | ## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Classification of Forecasting Techniques Forecasting techniques in the KTU framework are classified into two principal paradigms: #### A. Qualitative Techniques (Judgmental) Used when historical data is **scarce, unreliable, or when launching new products**. > [!NOTE] > - **Delphi Method:** Iterative anonymous expert panel consensus. > - **Market Research:** Surveys, focus groups, customer interviews. > - **Sales Force Composite:** Aggregating field sales personnel estimates. > - **Executive Opinion:** Top management gut-feel estimates. #### B. Quantitative Techniques (Statistical) Used when **sufficient historical data is available**. 1. **Time-Series Models** — assume demand is a function of time alone. 2. **Causal / Associative Models** — demand is a function of external variables (e.g., price, advertising, GDP). 3. **Machine Learning / AI Models** — Neural Networks, Random Forests, LSTM (advanced, beyond core KTU). --- ### 2.2 Time-Series Decomposition (Foundation Concept) Every time-series demand can be decomposed into four components: $$ D_t = L_t + T_t + C_t + S_t + \epsilon_t $$ Where: - $D_t$ = Actual demand at time $t$ - $L_t$ = **Level** (average value around which demand fluctuates) - $T_t$ = **Trend** (long-term upward or downward movement) - $C_t$ = **Cyclical component** (multi-year business cycles, e.g., recession) - $S_t$ = **Seasonal component** (recurring intra-year patterns, e.g., Onam sales) - $\epsilon_t$ = **Random / Irregular noise** (unexplained stochastic variation) **Two decomposition models:** - **Additive Model:** $D_t = L_t + T_t + S_t + \epsilon_t$ — used when seasonal variation is **constant in magnitude**. - **Multiplicative Model:** $D_t = L_t \times T_t \times S_t \times \epsilon_t$ — used when seasonal variation is **proportional to the level** of demand. --- ### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Master these formulas — they account for ~70% of numerical problems in KTU Module 2 exams.** | # | Technique | Formula | Key Parameter | Unit / Range | |---|-----------|---------|---------------|--------------| | 1 | **Simple Moving Average (SMA)** | $F_{t+1} = \dfrac{1}{n} \sum_{i=t-n+1}^{t} D_i$ | $n$ = number of periods | $n \in \mathbb{Z}^+$ | | 2 | **Weighted Moving Average (WMA)** | $F_{t+1} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i D_{t-i+1}$ | $w_i$ = weights | $\sum w_i = 1$ | | 3 | **Single Exponential Smoothing (SES)** | $F_{t+1} = \alpha D_t + (1-\alpha) F_t$ | $\alpha$ = smoothing constant | $0 \le \alpha \le 1$ | | 4 | **Trend-Adjusted Exponential Smoothing (Holt's Method)** | $L_t = \alpha D_t + (1-\alpha)(L_{t-1} + T_{t-1})$
$T_t = \beta(L_t - L_{t-1}) + (1-\beta) T_{t-1}$
$F_{t+m} = L_t + m \cdot T_t$ | $\alpha, \beta$ | $0 \le \alpha, \beta \le 1$ | | 5 | **Trend & Seasonality (Holt-Winters)** | Adds seasonal index $S_t$ to Holt's model | $\gamma$ for seasonality | $0 \le \gamma \le 1$ | | 6 | **Mean Absolute Deviation (MAD)** | $MAD = \dfrac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \vert D_i - F_i \vert$ | Lower is better | Units of $D$ | | 7 | **Mean Squared Error (MSE)** | $MSE = \dfrac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} (D_i - F_i)^2$ | Penalizes large errors | Squared units | | 8 | **Mean Absolute Percentage Error (MAPE)** | $MAPE = \dfrac{100}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left\vert \dfrac{D_i - F_i}{D_i} \right\vert$ | Scale-independent | Percentage | | 9 | **Tracking Signal** | $TS = \dfrac{\sum (D_i - F_i)}{MAD}$ | Bias detection | $\vert TS \vert \le 4$ acceptable | | 10 | **Linear Regression (Causal)** | $D = a + bX$ | $b = \dfrac{n\sum XD - \sum X \sum D}{n \sum X^2 - (\sum X)^2}$ | Coefficient | | 11 | **Seasonal Index** | $SI_j = \dfrac{\bar{D}_j}{\bar{D}}$ | $j$ = season number | Around 1.0 | | 12 | **Forecasting Accuracy (Theil's U)** | $U = \dfrac{\sqrt{\sum (F_{t+1} - D_{t+1})^2}}{\sqrt{\sum D_{t+1}^2} + \sqrt{\sum D_t^2}}$ | $U < 1$ = better than naive | $0 \le U \le 2$ | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner Trap:** In `WMA`, weights MUST sum to 1. Students often forget this normalization and lose **2–3 marks** per problem. --- ### 2.4 Selection Logic: Which Method to Use When? | Condition | Recommended Method | KTU Rationale | |-----------|-------------------|---------------| | Stationary data, no trend/seasonality | **SES** | Simple, low parameter overhead | | Trend present, no seasonality | **Holt's Linear Method** | Two-parameter smoothing | | Trend + Seasonality | **Holt-Winters** | Industry standard for FMCG | | Long stable history, no trend | **Moving Average** | Simplest baseline | | Demand depends on external variable (price, ad spend) | **Linear Regression / Causal Models** | Captures cause-effect | | New product launch, no data | **Qualitative (Delphi, Jury of Opinion)** | Data-independent | --- ### 2.5 Aligning Forecasts with Production Schedules The translation of forecast into production schedule is governed by the **Hierarchy of Planning**: > **Long-range Forecast (1–5 yrs) → Aggregate Planning → Master Production Schedule (MPS) → Short-range Forecasting (weeks/days) → Shop Floor Scheduling** Key alignment principles: 1. **Time-Phased Planning:** Break annual forecast into monthly, weekly, daily buckets. 2. **Lot-Sizing:** Convert net requirements into discrete production lots (EOQ, L4L, FOQ). 3. **Capacity Reconciliation:** Verify that the forecast-derived MPS does not exceed plant capacity. 4. **Safety Stock & Buffer Planning:** Hedge against forecast error using $SS = z \cdot \sigma_{LT}$. 5. **Rolling Forecast Updates:** Continuously refresh forecast with a "frozen horizon" (e.g., first 3 months frozen, last 9 months re-estimated). ## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Worked Example 1: Simple Moving Average (SMA) > **Problem:** Quarterly demand for an automotive spare part (in hundreds of units) for the last 4 quarters is: 120, 135, 128, 142. Using a **3-period Simple Moving Average**, forecast demand for Quarter 5. Also compute the **MAD** for the in-sample periods. #### Step 1 — State the SMA formula $$ F_{t+1} = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=t-n+1}^{t} D_i $$ With $n = 3$, we use the **last 3 actual demands** to predict the next. #### Step 2 — Compute Forecast for Q4 using Q1, Q2, Q3 $$ F_4 = \frac{D_1 + D_2 + D_3}{3} = \frac{120 + 135 + 128}{3} = \frac{383}{3} \approx 127.67 $$ #### Step 3 — Compute Forecast for Q5 using Q2, Q3, Q4 $$ F_5 = \frac{D_2 + D_3 + D_4}{3} = \frac{135 + 128 + 142}{3} = \frac{405}{3} = 135 $$ > **Forecast for Q5 = 135 hundred units (i.e., 13,500 units).** #### Step 4 — Compute Forecast Error and MAD | Quarter | Actual $D_t$ | Forecast $F_t$ | Error $e_t = D_t - F_t$ | $\vert e_t \vert$ | |---------|--------------|----------------|---------------------|----------------| | 1 | 120 | — | — | — | | 2 | 135 | — | — | — | | 3 | 128 | — | — | — | | 4 | 142 | 127.67 | $+14.33$ | $14.33$ | $$ MAD = \frac{1}{1} \sum \vert e_t \vert = 14.33 $$ > **Insight:** A high MAD relative to the mean demand (≈131) signals the need for a **trend-aware model** like Holt's method. The SMA underperforms in the presence of a visible upward trend. --- ### 3.2 Worked Example 2: Single Exponential Smoothing (SES) > **Problem:** Given actual demand values $D_1 = 200$, $D_2 = 220$, $D_3 = 210$, $D_4 = 230$, and $\alpha = 0.3$ with initial forecast $F_1 = 200$. Compute forecasts for periods 2, 3, 4, 5 and the MAD. #### Step 1 — Recursive SES formula $$ F_{t+1} = \alpha D_t + (1-\alpha) F_t $$ Substituting $\alpha = 0.3$: $$ F_{t+1} = 0.3 D_t + 0.7 F_t $$ #### Step 2 — Period-by-period computation **Period 2:** $$ F_2 = 0.3 \times 200 + 0.7 \times 200 = 60 + 140 = 200 $$ **Period 3:** $$ F_3 = 0.3 \times 220 + 0.7 \times 200 = 66 + 140 = 206 $$ **Period 4:** $$ F_4 = 0.3 \times 210 + 0.7 \times 206 = 63 + 144.2 = 207.2 $$ **Period 5 (the forecast for next period):** $$ F_5 = 0.3 \times 230 + 0.7 \times 207.2 = 69 + 145.04 = 214.04 $$ > **Forecast for Period 5 ≈ 214 units.** #### Step 3 — MAD calculation | $t$ | $D_t$ | $F_t$ | $e_t = D_t - F_t$ | $\vert e_t \vert$ | |-----|-------|-------|------------------|------------------| | 2 | 220 | 200 | $+20$ | 20 | | 3 | 210 | 206 | $+4$ | 4 | | 4 | 230 | 207.2 | $+22.8$ | 22.8 | $$ MAD = \frac{20 + 4 + 22.8}{3} = \frac{46.8}{3} = 15.6 $$ #### Step 4 — Selection of optimal $\alpha$ (KTU often asks this) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Theory:** Lower $\alpha$ → smoother forecast, more lag in reacting to changes. Higher $\alpha$ → more responsive, but noisier. **Optimal $\alpha$ minimizes MSE/MAD** — typically found by grid search over $\alpha \in \{0.1, 0.2, \ldots, 0.9\}$. --- ### 3.3 Worked Example 3: Holt's Linear Trend Method > **Problem:** Compute the forecast for periods 4, 5, and 6 using Holt's method with $\alpha = 0.4$, $\beta = 0.3$, $L_1 = 50$, $T_1 = 5$, given demand $D_2 = 55$, $D_3 = 60$, $D_4 = 58$. #### Step 1 — Holt's two recursion equations $$ L_t = \alpha D_t + (1-\alpha)(L_{t-1} + T_{t-1}) $$ $$ T_t = \beta(L_t - L_{t-1}) + (1-\beta) T_{t-1} $$ #### Step 2 — Compute for t = 2 $$ L_2 = 0.4(55) + 0.6(50 + 5) = 22 + 33 = 55 $$ $$ T_2 = 0.3(55 - 50) + 0.7(5) = 1.5 + 3.5 = 5.0 $$ #### Step 3 — Compute for t = 3 $$ L_3 = 0.4(60) + 0.6(55 + 5) = 24 + 36 = 60 $$ $$ T_3 = 0.3(60 - 55) + 0.7(5) = 1.5 + 3.5 = 5.0 $$ #### Step 4 — Compute for t = 4 $$ L_4 = 0.4(58) + 0.6(60 + 5) = 23.2 + 39 = 62.2 $$ $$ T_4 = 0.3(62.2 - 60) + 0.7(5) = 0.66 + 3.5 = 4.16 $$ #### Step 5 — Forecast for periods ahead The Holt forecast $m$ periods into the future: $$ F_{t+m} = L_t + m \cdot T_t $$ At the end of period 4: $$ F_5 = L_4 + 1 \cdot T_4 = 62.2 + 4.16 = 66.36 $$ $$ F_6 = L_4 + 2 \cdot T_4 = 62.2 + 8.32 = 70.52 $$ > **Forecasts: $F_5 = 66.36$, $F_6 = 70.52$** --- ### 3.4 Worked Example 4: Linear Regression (Causal Forecasting) > **Problem:** A company wants to forecast demand based on advertising spend (₹ in lakhs). Data: $\sum X = 50$, $\sum Y = 800$, $\sum XY = 5400$, $\sum X^2 = 350$, $n = 6$. Find the regression equation and forecast demand for ad spend = ₹12 lakhs. #### Step 1 — Compute slope $b$ $$ b = \frac{n \sum XY - \sum X \sum Y}{n \sum X^2 - (\sum X)^2} $$ Substituting: $$ b = \frac{6(5400) - (50)(800)}{6(350) - (50)^2} = \frac{32400 - 40000}{2100 - 2500} = \frac{-7600}{-400} = 19 $$ #### Step 2 — Compute intercept $a$ $$ \bar{X} = \frac{50}{6} \approx 8.333, \quad \bar{Y} = \frac{800}{6} \approx 133.33 $$ $$ a = \bar{Y} - b\bar{X} = 133.33 - 19(8.333) = 133.33 - 158.33 = -25 $$ #### Step 3 — Regression equation $$ D = -25 + 19X $$ #### Step 4 — Forecast for $X = 12$ $$ D = -25 + 19(12) = -25 + 228 = 203 \text{ units} $$ > **Forecasted demand = 203 units for ad spend of ₹12 lakhs.** #### Step 5 — Interpretation (Important for KTU theory) > [!TIP] > **Interpretation:** A 1 lakh increase in advertising spend is associated with a 19-unit increase in demand, *holding all other factors constant*. The negative intercept ($a = -25$) has no physical meaning — it is a mathematical artifact of linear extrapolation. --- ### 3.5 Comparative Analytical Matrix: Choosing the Best Method | Criterion | SMA | WMA | SES | Holt's | Regression | |-----------|-----|-----|-----|--------|------------| | Trend handling | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | ✅ | ✅ | | Seasonality handling | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ (Holt-Winters ✅) | Manual dummy vars | | Data requirement | Low | Low | Low | Medium | High | | Computational complexity | Very low | Very low | Low | Medium | High | | Responsiveness to recent data | Low | Tunable | Tunable via $\alpha$ | Tunable | N/A | | Best for noisy data | Moderate | High | High | Moderate | Low | | KTU exam frequency | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ | ⭐⭐⭐ | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ | ⭐⭐⭐ | ## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Mermaid Flow: Demand Forecasting Process Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Historical Sales Data] --> B[Data Cleaning and Validation] C[Market Intelligence] --> B D[External Variables: Price, GDP, Weather] --> B B --> E{Pattern Recognition} E -->|Trend Present| F[Apply Holt Method] E -->|Seasonality Present| G[Apply Holt Winters] E -->|Stationary Data| H[Apply SES or MA] E -->|Causal Relationship| I[Apply Linear Regression] F --> J[Generate Forecast Output] G --> J H --> J I --> J J --> K[Compute MAD, MAPE, MSE] K --> L{Forecast Acceptable Accuracy} L -->|Yes| M[Convert to Master Production Schedule] L -->|No| N[Adjust Alpha Beta Parameters] N --> F N --> G N --> H M --> O[Production Planning and Inventory Decisions] ``` > **Figure 1:** The complete demand forecasting pipeline as per KTU 2024 SCM framework — from data ingestion to production schedule alignment. --- ### 4.2 Mermaid Block: Forecast-to-Production Alignment ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Annual Strategic Forecast] --> B[Aggregate Production Plan] B --> C[Master Production Schedule MPS] C --> D[Material Requirements Planning MRP] D --> E[Shop Floor Scheduling] E --> F[Capacity Check] F -->|Capacity OK| G[Release to Production] F -->|Capacity Overload| H[Adjust MPS or Add Overtime] C --> I[Short Term Forecast Update Weekly] I --> C G --> J[Finished Goods Inventory] J --> K[Distribution and Customer Delivery] ``` > **Figure 2:** Closed-loop feedback between forecast updates and production execution. The dashed feedback is the **rolling forecast** mechanism central to S&OP. --- ### 4.3 Mermaid Architecture: Forecast Error Tracking & Bias Control ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Actual Demand Observed] --> B[Compare with Forecast] B --> C[Compute Error: Et = Dt minus Ft] C --> D[Compute Running Sum of Errors] C --> E[Compute MAD] D --> F[Tracking Signal: TS = SumE divided by MAD] E --> F F --> G{Absolute TS greater than 4} G -->|Yes| H[Forecast Model is Biased] H --> I[Recalibrate Alpha Beta or Switch Model] G -->|No| J[Forecast Model Acceptable] J --> K[Continue with Current Model] ``` > **Figure 3:** The Tracking Signal control loop. KTU students must remember the threshold $\vert TS \vert \le 4$ as a control limit (analogous to ±3σ in SPC). --- ### 4.4 Mermaid Decision Tree: Forecast Method Selection ```mermaid flowchart TD Start[Choose Forecasting Method] --> Q1{Historical Data Available} Q1 -->|No| Q2[Use Qualitative: Delphi, Jury of Opinion, Market Research] Q1 -->|Yes| Q3{Trend Present} Q3 -->|No| Q4{Seasonality Present} Q4 -->|No| Q5[Use Simple Moving Average or SES] Q4 -->|Yes| Q6[Use Holt Winters Seasonal Method] Q3 -->|Yes| Q7{Seasonality Present} Q7 -->|No| Q8[Use Holt Linear Trend Method] Q7 -->|Yes| Q9[Use Holt Winters Multiplicative or Additive] Q9 --> End[Optimal Forecast] Q8 --> End Q6 --> End Q5 --> End Q2 --> End ``` > **Figure 4:** A practical decision-tree that mirrors the method-selection logic taught in KTU Module 2. ## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **Distinguish between qualitative and quantitative forecasting techniques. Give two examples of each.** **Model Answer:** > **Qualitative Forecasting** relies on judgment, opinion, and expert intuition rather than numerical data. It is used when historical data is unavailable (e.g., new product launch, disruptive technology entry). > - Examples: (i) **Delphi Method** — iterative anonymous expert consensus. (ii) **Sales Force Composite** — aggregating estimates from field sales staff. > **Quantitative Forecasting** uses mathematical/statistical models applied to historical numerical data to project future demand. > - Examples: (i) **Moving Average** — averages past $n$ periods. (ii) **Exponential Smoothing** — weighted average with exponentially decaying weights. > **Key Distinction:** Qualitative is *subjective* and data-light; quantitative is *objective* and data-intensive. In practice, mature firms use a **hybrid approach** blending both. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** **What is the Tracking Signal in forecasting? State the rule of thumb for accepting a forecast based on it.** **Model Answer:** The **Tracking Signal (TS)** is a measure used to detect **forecast bias** — whether the forecast is consistently over-predicting or under-predicting actual demand. It is calculated as: $$ TS = \frac{\text{Running Sum of Forecast Errors (RSFE)}}{\text{Mean Absolute Deviation (MAD)}} $$ $$ TS = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} (D_i - F_i)}{MAD} $$ > **Acceptance Rule (KTU Standard):** A forecast is considered **acceptable** if $\vert TS \vert \le 4$. Values beyond ±4 indicate a **biased forecast** requiring recalibration (e.g., changing the smoothing constant $\alpha$, switching model, or adding trend/seasonality components). > If $TS > +4$ → forecast is consistently **under-predicting** (raise forecasts). > If $TS < -4$ → forecast is consistently **over-predicting** (lower forecasts). --- ### PART B — Full-Length 14-Mark Questions (ESE Pattern with Internal Choice) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** > - **Step 1 (1 mark):** Write the formula explicitly — do not skip. > - **Step 2 (2–3 marks):** Tabulate actual vs forecast vs error neatly. > - **Step 3 (3–4 marks):** Show all arithmetic; **rounding off too early costs marks**. > - **Step 4 (2–3 marks):** Conclude with a managerial interpretation (e.g., "the model is biased upward; recommend reducing $\alpha$"). > - **Common Loss:** Students often forget to **initialize $F_1$** or **normalize WMA weights** — this alone can cost 1–2 marks. --- #### ⭐ QUESTION A — 14 MARKS (Choose A or B) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Model Question]** > **(a) [7 Marks]** The monthly demand (in units) for a consumer durable over the last 6 months is: **240, 260, 280, 250, 270, 290**. Using **Single Exponential Smoothing** with $\alpha = 0.4$ and $F_1 = 240$: > (i) Generate forecasts for months 2 through 7. > (ii) Calculate MAD and MAPE. > (iii) Comment on the accuracy. > **(b) [7 Marks]** Explain with a neat diagram how the **Master Production Schedule (MPS)** is derived from the **demand forecast**. Discuss the role of **time fences** and **frozen horizon** in stabilizing the production plan. --- ### MODEL SOLUTION — QUESTION A(a) **Step 1 — SES Formula** $$ F_{t+1} = \alpha D_t + (1-\alpha) F_t = 0.4 D_t + 0.6 F_t $$ **Step 2 — Period-wise Forecasts** | Month $t$ | Actual $D_t$ | Forecast $F_t$ | Calculation | |-----------|--------------|----------------|-------------| | 1 | 240 | 240 | (Initial) | | 2 | 260 | 240 | $0.4(240)+0.6(240) = 240$ | | 3 | 280 | 248 | $0.4(260)+0.6(240) = 248$ | | 4 | 250 | 260.8 | $0.4(280)+0.6(248) = 260.8$ | | 5 | 270 | 256.48 | $0.4(250)+0.6(260.8) = 256.48$ | | 6 | 290 | 261.888 | $0.4(270)+0.6(256.48) = 261.888$ | | 7 | — | **272.933** | $0.4(290)+0.6(261.888) = 272.933$ | > **Forecast for Month 7 ≈ 273 units.** **Step 3 — MAD Calculation** | $t$ | $D_t$ | $F_t$ | Error $e_t = D_t - F_t$ | $\vert e_t \vert$ | $\left\vert \frac{e_t}{D_t} \right\vert \times 100$ | |-----|-------|-------|------------------------|------------------|-----------------------------------| | 2 | 260 | 240 | 20 | 20 | 7.69% | | 3 | 280 | 248 | 32 | 32 | 11.43% | | 4 | 250 | 260.8 | -10.8 | 10.8 | 4.32% | | 5 | 270 | 256.48 | 13.52 | 13.52 | 5.01% | | 6 | 290 | 261.888 | 28.112 | 28.112 | 9.69% | | **Sum** | | | | **104.43** | **38.14%** | $$ MAD = \frac{104.43}{5} = 20.886 \text{ units} $$ $$ MAPE = \frac{38.14}{5} = 7.628\% $$ **Step 4 — Managerial Interpretation** > **MAPE ≈ 7.63%** is generally considered **good accuracy** in supply chain contexts (industry benchmark: <10% is acceptable, <5% is excellent). The forecast tracks the upward trend, though with **some lag** (e.g., at month 6, actual = 290, forecast = 261.9 — under-predicting by 28 units). > > **Recommendation:** Use a **trend-adjusted model (Holt's method)** to capture the upward bias, or increase $\alpha$ to 0.5–0.6 for higher responsiveness. --- ### MODEL SOLUTION — QUESTION A(b) **Deriving MPS from Demand Forecast:** The **Master Production Schedule (MPS)** is the time-phased, product-level production plan that translates the demand forecast into a feasible schedule of **what to make, when to make, and in what quantity**. **Process Flow:** 1. **Demand Forecast** (typically monthly, from SES/Holt-Winters) → 2. **Aggregate Production Plan** (family-level units, capacity-constrained) → 3. **Disaggregation** into individual end-items (Bill of Materials explosion) → 4. **MPS Time-Phased Schedule** (week-level or day-level planned orders) → 5. **Available-to-Promise (ATP) calculation** for confirmed customer orders. **Role of Time Fences:** The MPS horizon is divided into three time fences: - **Frozen Zone (Demand Time Fence):** Typically the first 1–2 periods. **No changes allowed** to MPS quantities — this stabilizes shop floor execution. - **Slushy Zone (Planning Time Fence):** Middle periods. **Limited changes** allowed, requiring approval of production planning manager. - **Liquid Zone (Flexible):** Outer periods. **Free to change** based on updated forecasts. **Why the Frozen Horizon Matters:** - Provides **stability** to suppliers (POs already placed). - Prevents **nervousness** in the supply chain (bullwhip effect mitigation). - Aligns with **capacity-constrained resources** that cannot be re-tooled frequently. > **KTU Tip:** Always include a labelled diagram (3 time fences marked on a horizontal time axis) — diagrams earn 1–2 easy marks in KTU valuation. --- #### ⭐ QUESTION B — 14 MARKS (Alternative Choice) **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question]** > **(a) [7 Marks]** Quarterly sales data for a product shows a clear seasonal pattern. The deseasonalized data follows a linear trend. Use the following information to forecast sales for the next 4 quarters using **Holt's Linear Trend Method**: > - Initial Level $L_1 = 100$, Initial Trend $T_1 = 5$ > - Smoothing constants: $\alpha = 0.3$, $\beta = 0.2$ > - Deseasonalized demand (Q1–Q4 of current year): $D_1 = 102$, $D_2 = 108$, $D_3 = 115$, $D_4 = 122$ > - Seasonal indices: $SI_1 = 0.85$, $SI_2 = 0.95$, $SI_3 = 1.10$, $SI_4 = 1.10$ > **(b) [7 Marks]** Discuss the **Aggregate Production Planning strategies** available to a manufacturer. How does the **level strategy** differ from the **chase strategy**? Which is more suitable for a KTU-region FMCG company producing packaged foods, and why? --- ### MODEL SOLUTION — QUESTION B(a) **Step 1 — Apply Holt's recursion on deseasonalized data** $$ L_t = \alpha D_t + (1-\alpha)(L_{t-1} + T_{t-1}) $$ $$ T_t = \beta(L_t - L_{t-1}) + (1-\beta) T_{t-1} $$ **Step 2 — Quarter-by-Quarter Computation** **t = 1:** $$ L_1 = 100, \quad T_1 = 5 \text{ (given)} $$ **t = 2:** $$ L_2 = 0.3(102) + 0.7(100 + 5) = 30.6 + 73.5 = 104.1 $$ $$ T_2 = 0.2(104.1 - 100) + 0.8(5) = 0.82 + 4 = 4.82 $$ **t = 3:** $$ L_3 = 0.3(108) + 0.7(104.1 + 4.82) = 32.4 + 76.244 = 108.644 $$ $$ T_3 = 0.2(108.644 - 104.1) + 0.8(4.82) = 0.9088 + 3.856 = 4.7648 $$ **t = 4:** $$ L_4 = 0.3(115) + 0.7(108.644 + 4.7648) = 34.5 + 79.386 = 113.886 $$ $$ T_4 = 0.2(113.886 - 108.644) + 0.8(4.7648) = 1.0484 + 3.8118 = 4.8602 $$ **Step 3 — Forecast Deseasonalized Demand for Next 4 Quarters** $$ F_{t+m} = L_4 + m \cdot T_4 = 113.886 + 4.8602 m $$ | Next Quarter | $m$ | Deseasonalized Forecast | Seasonal Index | Seasonalized Forecast | |--------------|-----|------------------------|----------------|----------------------| | Q1 next year | 1 | $113.886 + 4.86 = 118.75$ | 0.85 | **100.94** | | Q2 next year | 2 | $113.886 + 9.72 = 123.61$ | 0.95 | **117.43** | | Q3 next year | 3 | $113.886 + 14.58 = 128.47$ | 1.10 | **141.31** | | Q4 next year | 4 | $113.886 + 19.44 = 133.33$ | 1.10 | **146.66** | > **Seasonalized Forecasts: Q1 = 101, Q2 = 117, Q3 = 141, Q4 = 147 units.** **Step 4 — Validation Check** Sum of seasonal indices = $0.85 + 0.95 + 1.10 + 1.10 = 4.00$ → average = **1.00** ✅ (well-formed indices). --- ### MODEL SOLUTION — QUESTION B(b) **Aggregate Production Planning Strategies:** Aggregate planning determines the **overall production quantity, workforce levels, and inventory levels** to meet forecasted demand over a medium-term horizon (typically 3–18 months). **Key Strategies:** 1. **Level Strategy:** Production rate and workforce held **constant**. Demand fluctuations absorbed by **inventory buildup and stockout**. Stable employment, simple to administer. 2. **Chase Strategy:** Production rate **matches demand period-by-period**. Workforce hired/fired or overtime used. Low inventory, high labor cost variability. 3. **Mixed/Hybrid Strategy:** A combination — uses subcontracting, part-time workers, and inventory to balance. **Comparison Table:** | Dimension | Level Strategy | Chase Strategy | |-----------|---------------|----------------| | Inventory | High (build ahead) | Low | | Workforce | Stable | Fluctuating | | Hiring/Firing cost | Low | High | | Customer service | High (stock always available) | Risk of stockout | | Suitability | Stable demand, high skilled labor | Volatile demand, low skilled labor | **Recommendation for KTU-Region FMCG (packaged foods):** > **Recommendation: LEVEL strategy is preferable**, with seasonal pre-buildup before Onam/Vishu and monsoon lean-down. **Justification:** - **Consumer demand is relatively stable** with predictable peaks (Onam, Vishu, Christmas, school reopening). - **Perishable goods** (e.g., packaged snacks, dairy) cannot tolerate stockouts — level strategy ensures availability. - **Workforce stability** is valued in Kerala's labor culture; layoffs hurt brand reputation. - **Pre-build inventory in Q2** to meet Q3 (Onam) surge, then **draw down inventory** in Q4. > [!TIP] > **KTU Insight:** In board exams, always justify your choice with **2–3 specific reasons** tied to the industry context — generic answers lose marks. --- ### TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Module 2 — Demand Forecasting** **Core Definitions:** - ✅ Demand Forecasting = estimating future demand using historical data + methods. - ✅ Demand Planning = translating the forecast into procurement, production, and inventory actions. - ✅ Forecast Horizon = Short (≤3m), Medium (3–12m), Long (>1y). **Time-Series Decomposition:** - ✅ $D_t = L_t + T_t + C_t + S_t + \epsilon_t$ (additive form). - ✅ Multiplicative form: $D_t = L_t \times T_t \times S_t \times \epsilon_t$. **Forecasting Models — Master the Formulas:** - ✅ **SMA:** $F_{t+1} = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=t-n+1}^{t} D_i$ - ✅ **WMA:** $F_{t+1} = \sum w_i D_{t-i+1}$, with $\sum w_i = 1$. - ✅ **SES:** $F_{t+1} = \alpha D_t + (1-\alpha) F_t$ - ✅ **Holt's:** Two recursions for Level $L_t$ and Trend $T_t$. - ✅ **Regression:** $D = a + bX$, $b = \frac{n\sum XY - \sum X \sum Y}{n \sum X^2 - (\sum X)^2}$. **Error Metrics:** - ✅ **MAD** = $\frac{1}{n} \sum \vert D_i - F_i \vert$ - ✅ **MSE** = $\frac{1}{n} \sum (D_i - F_i)^2$ - ✅ **MAPE** = $\frac{100}{n} \sum \left\vert \frac{D_i - F_i}{D_i} \right\vert$ - ✅ **Tracking Signal** = $\frac{\text{RSFE}}{\text{MAD}}$; control limit $\pm 4$. **Forecast-to-Production Alignment:** - ✅ Hierarchy: Forecast → Aggregate Plan → MPS → MRP → Shop Floor Schedule. - ✅ **Time Fences:** Frozen / Slushy / Liquid zones in the MPS horizon. - ✅ **S&OP:** Monthly cross-functional reconciliation of forecast and supply. - ✅ **Safety Stock:** $SS = z \cdot \sigma_{LT} \cdot \sqrt{L}$, hedging forecast error. **Method Selection Rule of Thumb:** - ✅ No trend, no seasonality → **SMA or SES**. - ✅ Trend only → **Holt's Linear**. - ✅ Trend + Seasonality → **Holt-Winters**. - ✅ Causal relationship → **Regression / Econometric Models**. **Common Pitfalls to Avoid:** - ❌ Don't confuse MAD (units of D) with MAPE (percentage). - ❌ Don't forget to **normalize WMA weights** to sum = 1. - ❌ Don't set $\alpha$ too high (>0.7) for noisy data — forecast becomes erratic. - ❌ Don't use SMA when there is a clear trend — it will always lag. - ❌ Don't apply qualitative methods when 3+ years of clean data is available. **KTU Board Exam Strategy:** - 📌 Always tabulate: $t$, $D_t$, $F_t$, $e_t$, $\vert e_t \vert$, $\vert e_t/D_t \vert \times 100$. - 📌 Write the formula *before* substituting values. - 📌 End with a **managerial interpretation** — this is often worth 1–2 marks. - 📌 For 14-mark questions, allocate ~5 min for planning, ~25 min for solving, ~5 min for review. - 📌 In theory questions, a labelled diagram = free marks.

topics.title: Inventory Management: Principles of Inventory Control and Optimization Strategies in the Automotive Supply Chain
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT753 - SUPPLY CHAIN MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Inventory Management: Principles of Inventory Control and Optimization Strategies in the Automotive Supply Chain

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Inventory Management** is the strategic and operational process of ordering, storing, tracking, and controlling a firm's inventory levels to meet customer demand while minimizing the total cost of inventory, which includes **holding costs**, **ordering costs**, and **shortage (stockout) costs**.

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme** framework (aligned with **NEP 2020** and **Outcome-Based Education**), inventory management is treated as a *core decision-making subsystem* of Supply Chain Management (SCM) that directly influences the firm's **service level**, **working capital**, and **supply chain resilience**.

### 1.1.1 Types of Inventory in an Automotive Plant

| Type | Description | Automotive Example |
|------|-------------|--------------------|
| **Raw Materials (RM)** | Unprocessed inputs | Steel coils, aluminum sheets, rubber |
| **Work-In-Progress (WIP)** | Partially completed items | Painted but unassembled car bodies |
| **Finished Goods (FG)** | Ready for dispatch | Fully built Maruti, Hyundai, Tata vehicles |
| **MRO (Maintenance, Repair, Operations)** | Consumables for plant | Lubricants, machine spares, cutting tools |
| **Pipeline / Transit Inventory** | Goods in transit | Components shipped from Tier-2 to Tier-1 supplier |

### 1.1.2 Core Functions of Inventory

> [!NOTE]
> The four fundamental purposes of holding inventory, often remembered as the **"4 D's"**:
> 1. **Decoupling** operations (buffer between stages)
> 2. **Demand & Supply Buffering** (uncertainty absorption)
> 3. **Economies of Scale** (lot sizing)
> 4. **Hedge against price fluctuations** (speculative motive)

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy: Inventory is Like a Water Tank

Imagine your **home water tank** on the roof:
- The **water level** = inventory level
- **Taps running** = customer demand draining inventory
- **Motor pump refilling** = supplier replenishment orders
- **Overflow risk** = excess holding cost
- **Empty tank risk** = stockout / lost sales

A smart homeowner doesn't keep the tank **completely full** (waste of money and space) nor **completely empty** (risk of shortage). They maintain an **optimal level** based on:
- How much water the family uses per day (**demand rate**)
- How often the motor is switched on (**ordering frequency**)
- The cost of electricity per pump cycle (**ordering cost**)
- The risk of motor failure (**supply uncertainty**)

This is *exactly* what inventory management does for an automotive OEM (Original Equipment Manufacturer) like Maruti Suzuki — they don't want a factory overflowing with brake discs, nor do they want the assembly line stopping because a brake disc is missing.

---

## 1.3 Why Inventory Management is Critical in the Automotive Supply Chain

The automotive industry is the **gold standard** for studying inventory management because of:
- **Extreme SKU proliferation** (a single car model has 20,000+ parts)
- **Multi-tier supplier networks** (OEM → Tier 1 → Tier 2 → Tier 3)
- **High capital tied up** in steel, electronics, and components
- **Synchronous production** (assembly lines run 24×7; any missing part halts production)
- **Mass customization** pressure (each customer wants different variants)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Industry Statistic (Industry Standard Benchmark):** A typical automotive OEM holds **30–60 days** of finished goods inventory but only **2–8 hours** of WIP at each station (a hallmark of lean/JIT systems).

---

## 1.4 Cost Components of Inventory

The three principal cost drivers are:

1. **Holding Cost (H)** — Cost of carrying one unit for one year
   - Includes warehousing, insurance, depreciation, obsolescence, opportunity cost of capital
   - Typically **20%–30% of item value per year** in automotive components
2. **Ordering Cost (S)** — Fixed cost per purchase order
   - Includes purchase requisition, transportation, inspection, payment processing
3. **Shortage Cost (Stockout Cost)** — Penalty for unmet demand
   - Lost sales, production line stoppage, customer dissatisfaction

---

## 1.5 KTU 2024 Course Outcome (CO) Mapping

| CO ID | Course Outcome | Bloom's Level |
|-------|----------------|---------------|
| CO2 | Apply inventory control techniques in real-world supply chains | Apply (L3) |
| CO3 | Analyze optimization strategies for automotive networks | Analyze (L4) |
| CO4 | Evaluate lean and agile inventory practices | Evaluate (L5) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Principles of Inventory Control

The **seven foundational principles** taught in the KTU syllabus are:

### Principle 1: The Inventory Trade-off
- Higher inventory → lower stockout risk but higher holding cost
- Lower inventory → lower holding cost but higher stockout + ordering cost
- The *optimal* point minimizes **Total Cost (TC)**

### Principle 2: Pareto / ABC Analysis
- **A Items** (≈20% of SKUs, ≈80% of value) → tight control
- **B Items** (≈30% of SKUs, ≈15% of value) → moderate control
- **C Items** (≈50% of SKUs, ≈5% of value) → loose control

### Principle 3: Continuous Review vs. Periodic Review
- **Continuous Review (Q-system):** Monitor stock continuously; order fixed Q when level drops to ROP
- **Periodic Review (P-system):** Review at fixed intervals; order up to target level S

### Principle 4: Deterministic vs. Probabilistic Models
- **Deterministic** — known, constant demand (EOQ, EPQ)
- **Probabilistic** — variable demand (Stochastic models with safety stock)

### Principle 5: Single vs. Multi-Echelon Inventory
- **Single-echelon** — only one stage optimized
- **Multi-echelon** — entire network (OEM + Tier 1 + Tier 2) optimized jointly

### Principle 6: Independent vs. Dependent Demand
- **Independent** — finished cars, spare parts
- **Dependent** — sub-assemblies tied to finished goods bill of materials (BOM)

### Principle 7: Lean vs. Agile Inventory
- **Lean** — minimize inventory (Toyota Production System)
- **Agile** — buffer inventory for responsiveness (luxury/niche automotive)

---

## 2.2 The Economic Order Quantity (EOQ) Model — Wilson's Formula

EOQ is the **most frequently tested concept** in KTU examinations.

### Assumptions of the Classical EOQ Model
1. Demand is **constant and known (D units/year)**
2. Lead time (L) is **constant and known**
3. Order quantity is **received instantaneously** (infinite replenishment rate)
4. **No shortages** are permitted
5. Holding cost (H) per unit per year and ordering cost (S) per order are **constant**

### Derivation Setup
- Let $Q$ = order quantity
- Number of orders per year = $D / Q$
- Average inventory = $Q / 2$

### Total Annual Cost Function
The total relevant cost has two components:

$$TC(Q) = \underbrace{\text{Ordering Cost}}_{\text{More orders, smaller Q}} + \underbrace{\text{Holding Cost}}_{\text{More inventory, larger Q}}$$

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains **all the formulas** you must memorize for the KTU ESE and internal assessments.

| # | Formula Name | Mathematical Expression | Units / Notation | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **EOQ (Wilson)** | $Q^{*} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2DS}{H}}$ | $D$=demand/yr, $S$=₹/order, $H$=₹/unit/yr | Static, deterministic demand |
| 2 | **Total Cost (EOQ)** | $TC^{*} = \sqrt{2DSH}$ | Rupees per year | Minimum annual cost |
| 3 | **Number of Orders** | $N = \dfrac{D}{Q^{*}}$ | Orders/year | Order frequency |
| 4 | **Cycle Time (T)** | $T = \dfrac{Q^{*}}{D}$ | Years | Time between orders |
| 5 | **Reorder Point (ROP)** | $ROP = d \cdot L$ | Units | When to order |
| 6 | **Safety Stock (SS)** | $SS = z \cdot \sigma_{LT}$ | Units | Buffer for uncertainty |
| 7 | **ROP with SS** | $ROP = d \cdot L + z \cdot \sigma_{LT}$ | Units | Stochastic demand |
| 8 | **Avg. Inventory** | $\bar{I} = \dfrac{Q}{2} + SS$ | Units | Working capital |
| 9 | **Inventory Turnover** | $ITR = \dfrac{COGS}{\text{Avg. Inv.}}$ | Times/year | Efficiency |
| 10 | **Days of Supply** | $DoS = \dfrac{\text{Avg. Inv.}}{D / 365}$ | Days | Liquidity measure |
| 11 | **EOQ w/ quantity discount** | Use breakpoints, find min TC | ₹/year | Vendor discounts |
| 12 | **Production Order Qty (EPQ)** | $Q_{p}^{*} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2DS}{H \cdot (1 - d/p)}}$ | Units | Finite production rate p |
| 13 | **Backorder EOQ** | $Q^{*} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2DS}{H} \cdot \dfrac{H + B}{B}}$ | Units | Allowed backorders (B) |
| 14 | **Pipeline Inventory** | $I_{p} = d \cdot L_{transit}$ | Units | Goods in transit |
| 15 | **Carrying Cost %** | $i = \dfrac{H}{C \cdot u}$ | Fraction | Cost of capital tied up |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL TABLE SYNTAX RULE:** Notice that absolute value bars are written as `\vert` (not the raw `\|` character) to keep the markdown table valid. The same applies to `$\vert x \vert$` in any table cell.

---

## 2.4 ABC Analysis — Detailed Logic

The **80/20 rule** applied to inventory:

- **A Items** (≈10–20% of SKUs) → Annual consumption value ≈ 70–80% → *tight control*, daily review, accurate records
- **B Items** (≈20–30% of SKUs) → ≈ 15–25% value → *moderate control*, weekly review
- **C Items** (≈50–70% of SKUs) → ≈ 5–10% value → *loose control*, bulk ordering, simple controls

> [!TIP]
> **Automotive Example:** In a Maruti assembly line:
> - **A Items:** Engine blocks, transmissions, electronic control units
> - **B Items:** Brake pads, alternators, headlamps
> - **C Items:** Washers, screws, small fasteners

---

## 2.5 Optimization Strategies in the Automotive Supply Chain

### Strategy 1: Just-In-Time (JIT)
- Components arrive **exactly when needed**, minimizing WIP inventory
- Originated at **Toyota Production System (TPS)** — Taichi Ohno
- **Discipline:** Heijunka (level scheduling), Kanban (signal cards), Jidoka (autonomation)

### Strategy 2: Vendor-Managed Inventory (VMI)
- **Supplier** monitors OEM's stock and replenishes autonomously
- Common for Bosch, Continental, ZF Friedrichshafen supplying to VW, Toyota, Tata

### Strategy 3: Cross-Docking
- Incoming parts are **sorted at a hub** and dispatched to assembly line **without storage**
- Reduces warehouse dwell time to **under 24 hours**

### Strategy 4: Milk Runs
- A single truck visits **multiple Tier-2/Tier-3 suppliers** in a circular route
- Brings parts to OEM just in time; cost-efficient for low-volume components

### Strategy 5: Hub-and-Spoke for Spare Parts
- Central warehouse (hub) feeds regional distribution centers (spokes)
- Reduces response time for after-sales service

### Strategy 6: Hub-and-Spoke vs. Point-to-Point (KTU Frequently Tested)

| Criterion | Point-to-Point | Hub-and-Spoke |
|---|---|---|
| Transit time | High (multiple legs) | Lower (consolidated) |
| Cost | High for low volume | Lower per unit |
| Inventory | Spread out | Consolidated at hub |
| Risk | Distributed | Single point of failure |
| Use Case | High-value, urgent | Bulk, scheduled |

### Strategy 7: The Bullwhip Effect
- Demand variability **amplifies** upstream from OEM to Tier 3
- Mitigation: **VMI, CPFR, lead time reduction, order smoothing**

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the EOQ Formula (Wilson's 1913 Model)

We begin with the total cost as a function of order quantity $Q$:

### Step 1: Express the Ordering Cost Component

The number of orders placed in a year is the total annual demand $D$ divided by the size of each order $Q$:

$$N = \frac{D}{Q}$$

Therefore, the **annual ordering cost** is the number of orders times the cost per order $S$:

$$TC_{\text{order}} = \frac{D}{Q} \cdot S$$

### Step 2: Express the Holding Cost Component

When the order of size $Q$ arrives, inventory jumps to $Q$. As demand is consumed uniformly, inventory linearly drops to zero just before the next order arrives. The **average inventory** is therefore:

$$\bar{I} = \frac{Q}{2}$$

The **annual holding cost** is the average inventory times the per-unit holding cost $H$:

$$TC_{\text{hold}} = \frac{Q}{2} \cdot H$$

### Step 3: Total Cost Function

Adding the two cost components:

$$TC(Q) = \frac{D \cdot S}{Q} + \frac{Q \cdot H}{2}$$

### Step 4: Differentiate $TC(Q)$ with Respect to $Q$

We use first-order calculus to find the minimum:

$$\frac{d\,TC}{d\,Q} = -\frac{D \cdot S}{Q^{2}} + \frac{H}{2}$$

### Step 5: Set the Derivative to Zero (Optimality Condition)

$$-\frac{D \cdot S}{Q^{2}} + \frac{H}{2} = 0$$

Rearranging:

$$\frac{H}{2} = \frac{D \cdot S}{Q^{2}}$$

$$Q^{2} = \frac{2 \cdot D \cdot S}{H}$$

$$Q^{*} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \cdot D \cdot S}{H}}$$

### Step 6: Verify Minimum (Second-Order Condition)

$$\frac{d^{2}\,TC}{d\,Q^{2}} = \frac{2 \cdot D \cdot S}{Q^{3}} > 0 \quad \text{(Convex, hence a minimum)}$$

### Step 7: Substitute $Q^{*}$ Back into TC to Get Minimum Total Cost

$$TC^{*} = \frac{D \cdot S}{\sqrt{2DS/H}} + \frac{\sqrt{2DS/H} \cdot H}{2}$$

After algebraic simplification:

$$TC^{*} = \sqrt{2 \cdot D \cdot S \cdot H}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Note:** Both $Q^{*}$ and $TC^{*}$ are *closed-form* — meaning you can compute the optimal order quantity and the minimum total cost directly without iteration. The examiner will test this derivation directly in the 14-mark question.

---

## 3.2 Reorder Point (ROP) Derivation with Safety Stock

### Step 1: Expected Demand During Lead Time
If daily demand is $d$ units and lead time is $L$ days, the expected demand during lead time is:

$$\mu_{LT} = d \cdot L$$

### Step 2: Standard Deviation of Demand During Lead Time
If daily demand has standard deviation $\sigma_{d}$ and lead time variance is $\sigma_{L}$:

$$\sigma_{LT} = \sqrt{L \cdot \sigma_{d}^{2} + d^{2} \cdot \sigma_{L}^{2}}$$

### Step 3: Safety Stock with Desired Service Level
The safety stock needed to provide a cycle service level of $F(z) = 1 - \alpha$ is:

$$SS = z_{\alpha} \cdot \sigma_{LT}$$

### Step 4: Reorder Point

$$ROP = \mu_{LT} + SS = d \cdot L + z_{\alpha} \cdot \sigma_{LT}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The $z$-values for common service levels:
> - 90% → $z = 1.28$
> - 95% → $z = 1.65$
> - 97.5% → $z = 1.96$
> - 99% → $z = 2.33$
> - 99.9% → $z = 3.09$

---

## 3.3 EOQ Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

> **Problem:** A Maruti Suzuki vendor supplies **brake calipers** to the OEM. Annual demand $D = 12{,}000$ units. Cost per order $S = \text{₹}\,1500$. Holding cost $H = \text{₹}\,30$ per unit per year. Lead time $L = 10$ days. Find EOQ, $TC^{*}$, number of orders, cycle time, and ROP. Take 1 year = 300 working days.

### Solution — Step A: Compute EOQ

$$Q^{*} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \cdot 12000 \cdot 1500}{30}} = \sqrt{\frac{36{,}000{,}000}{30}} = \sqrt{1{,}200{,}000}$$

$$Q^{*} \approx 1095.45 \text{ units} \approx 1096 \text{ units}$$

### Solution — Step B: Minimum Total Cost

$$TC^{*} = \sqrt{2 \cdot 12000 \cdot 1500 \cdot 30} = \sqrt{1.08 \times 10^{9}} = \text{₹}\,32{,}863$$

### Solution — Step C: Number of Orders per Year

$$N = \frac{12000}{1095.45} \approx 10.95 \approx 11 \text{ orders/year}$$

### Solution — Step D: Cycle Time (in days)

$$T = \frac{1095.45}{12000} \times 300 \text{ days} \approx 27.4 \text{ days per cycle}$$

### Solution — Step E: Reorder Point

Daily demand $d = 12000 / 300 = 40$ units/day.

$$ROP = d \cdot L = 40 \times 10 = 400 \text{ units}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Always show units in the final answer** for full marks. KTU evaluators award 1 mark specifically for *unit consistency* in numerical problems.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: When to Use Which Inventory Model

| Situation | Best Model | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Constant demand, no shortages | Classical EOQ | Closed-form, simple |
| Constant demand, finite production rate | EPQ | Production is gradual, not instant |
| Quantity discount offered | Price-break EOQ | Min TC over discount slabs |
| Variable demand, fixed L | Safety stock with z | Probabilistic service level |
| Variable demand + variable L | $\sigma_{LT}$ formula | Both sources of variability |
| Allowed backorders | Backorder EOQ | Lower holding cost |
| Joint replenishment | Multi-item EOQ | Shared ordering cost |
| Spare parts (lumpy demand) | (s, S) or (R, S) policy | Min/Max levels |
| Auto spare, A items | Continuous review (Q, R) | Tight control |
| Auto raw material bulk | Periodic review (P) | Simpler admin |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 EOQ Cost Trade-off Curve (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

The total cost curve is a **U-shape** with the ordering cost curve decreasing (hyperbola) and the holding cost curve increasing (linear). The EOQ lies exactly at the **intersection** where the two costs are equal.

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeA[Order Quantity Q axis]
    nodeB[Total Cost Rupees axis]
    nodeC[Ordering Cost curve - decreasing hyperbola]
    nodeD[Holding Cost curve - increasing line]
    nodeE[Total Cost U-shaped curve - sum of C and D]
    nodeF[EOQ point - minimum of Total Cost]
    nodeG[Optimal Zone - flat bottom of U]
    nodeH[Over Ordering zone - high holding cost]
    nodeI[Under Ordering zone - high ordering cost]

    nodeA --> nodeC
    nodeA --> nodeD
    nodeC --> nodeE
    nodeD --> nodeE
    nodeE --> nodeF
    nodeF --> nodeG
    nodeG --> nodeH
    nodeG --> nodeI

    style nodeF fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style nodeE fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style nodeG fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
```

---

## 4.2 Multi-Tier Automotive Supply Chain Inventory Architecture

The diagram below maps the **flow of components, information, and inventory** across the OEM and its tiered suppliers. It illustrates where each inventory type resides and which optimization strategy is applied at each echelon.

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeO[OEM Assembly Plant - Maruti Suzuki Gurgaon]
    nodeT1a[Tier 1 Supplier - Bosch Fuel Injectors]
    nodeT1b[Tier 1 Supplier - Continental ECU]
    nodeT2a[Tier 2 Supplier - Steel Strip Manufacturer]
    nodeT2b[Tier 2 Supplier - Semiconductor Fab]
    nodeT3a[Tier 3 Supplier - Iron Ore Mine]
    nodeT3b[Tier 3 Supplier - Silicon Wafer Plant]
    nodeHUB[Central Hub Warehouse - Pune]
    nodeRDC1[Regional DC - North]
    nodeRDC2[Regional DC - South]
    nodeRDC3[Regional DC - East]
    nodeDEALER[Dealer Network - Aftermarket Spare Parts]

    nodeO -- Finished cars - low FG inventory JIT --> nodeDEALER
    nodeO -- Kanban signal every 4 hours --> nodeT1a
    nodeO -- VMI replenishment --> nodeT1b
    nodeT1a -- Milk run daily delivery --> nodeO
    nodeT1b -- Cross-dock 6 hour dwell --> nodeO
    nodeT1a -- VMI buffer at Tier 1 --> nodeT2a
    nodeT1b -- Safety stock 3 days --> nodeT2b
    nodeT2a -- Bulk EOQ quarterly --> nodeT3a
    nodeT2b -- Safety stock for long LT --> nodeT3b
    nodeHUB -- Spare parts for after sales --> nodeRDC1
    nodeHUB -- Spare parts for after sales --> nodeRDC2
    nodeHUB -- Spare parts for after sales --> nodeRDC3
    nodeRDC1 --> nodeDEALER
    nodeRDC2 --> nodeDEALER
    nodeRDC3 --> nodeDEALER

    style nodeO fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style nodeHUB fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style nodeDEALER fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style nodeT1a fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style nodeT1b fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
```

---

## 4.3 The Reorder Point (ROP) Decision Flow

This block-level flow shows the **logical sequence** of decision points in a continuous review (Q, R) inventory system. At each inventory check, the system compares the current position to the ROP and triggers an order when needed.

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeStart[Inventory Check Triggered - Continuous Review]
    nodeCurrent[Read Current Inventory Position IP]
    nodeCompare{Is IP less than or equal to ROP?}
    nodeNo[No Action - Wait for next check]
    nodeYes[Yes - Place Order of size Q star EOQ]
    nodeLead[Order En Route - Lead Time L days]
    nodeArrive[Inventory Replenished by Q units]
    nodeSSCheck{Safety Stock Buffer Active?}
    nodeSSYes[Absorb Demand Variability]
    nodeSSNo[Normal Replenishment]
    nodeLoop[Return to Inventory Check Loop]

    nodeStart --> nodeCurrent
    nodeCurrent --> nodeCompare
    nodeCompare -- No --> nodeNo
    nodeCompare -- Yes --> nodeYes
    nodeYes --> nodeLead
    nodeLead --> nodeArrive
    nodeArrive --> nodeSSCheck
    nodeSSCheck -- Yes --> nodeSSYes
    nodeSSCheck -- No --> nodeSSNo
    nodeSSYes --> nodeLoop
    nodeSSNo --> nodeLoop
    nodeNo --> nodeLoop

    style nodeYes fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style nodeCompare fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style nodeStart fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
```

---

## 4.4 ABC Analysis Classification Logic

The flowchart below shows the **decision logic** for classifying inventory items into A, B, or C categories based on the cumulative annual consumption value.

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeStart[Begin ABC Analysis on All SKUs]
    nodeCalc[Compute Annual Consumption Value equals Unit Cost times Annual Usage for each SKU]
    nodeSort[Sort SKUs in Descending Order of Value]
    nodeCum[Cumulative Percentage Calculation]
    nodeA{Top items covering 70 to 80 percent of cumulative value}
    nodeB{Next items covering 15 to 25 percent of cumulative value}
    nodeC{Remaining items covering 5 to 10 percent of cumulative value}
    nodeClassA[Class A - Tight Control - Daily Review - Continuous Review Q R - Low Safety Stock Tolerance]
    nodeClassB[Class B - Moderate Control - Weekly Review - Periodic P System - Medium Safety Stock]
    nodeClassC[Class C - Loose Control - Monthly Review - Bulk Two Bin System - High Safety Stock OK]
    nodeFinal[ABC Classification Complete - Apply Control Policies]

    nodeStart --> nodeCalc
    nodeCalc --> nodeSort
    nodeSort --> nodeCum
    nodeCum --> nodeA
    nodeA -- Yes --> nodeClassA
    nodeA -- No --> nodeB
    nodeB -- Yes --> nodeClassB
    nodeB -- No --> nodeC
    nodeC -- Yes --> nodeClassC
    nodeClassA --> nodeFinal
    nodeClassB --> nodeFinal
    nodeClassC --> nodeFinal

    style nodeClassA fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style nodeClassB fill:#FFD93D,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style nodeClassC fill:#6BCB77,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style nodeFinal fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> *Part A questions in the KTU ESE are designed to be answered in 5–7 sentences, each carrying 3 marks with 1 mark reserved for a precise definition and 2 marks for explanation/application.*

---

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

**Define inventory management. Explain its significance in the automotive supply chain with a suitable example.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Inventory management** is the systematic process of planning, ordering, storing, tracking, and controlling a firm's stock of raw materials, work-in-progress, finished goods, and MRO items to meet customer demand while minimizing total cost (holding + ordering + shortage).
>
> **Significance in the automotive supply chain (Example):** A typical passenger car has over **20,000 parts** sourced from multiple tiers of suppliers. For instance, Maruti Suzuki procures brake calipers from Bosch, ECUs from Continental, and glass from Asahi India. Effective inventory management ensures:
> - **Uninterrupted assembly line operation** (line stoppage costs ₹50–80 lakh/hour in some plants)
> - **Lower working capital lock-up** (Tata Motors targets inventory turnover of 8–10x)
> - **Customer service level** of 95%+ for spare parts
>
> Without it, a single missing bolt could halt an entire production shift, costing millions.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**Differentiate between continuous review and periodic review inventory systems. State which is more suitable for A-class automotive components.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Continuous Review (Q, R) | Periodic Review (P) |
|---|---|---|
| Trigger | Stock drops to ROP | Fixed time interval |
| Order size | Fixed (Q) | Variable (up to S) |
| Record keeping | Every transaction | Only at review |
| Safety stock | Lower | Higher |
| Suitable for | **A items (high value, tight control)** | C items (low value, bulk) |

For A-class automotive components such as **engine control units and transmissions**, the **continuous review (Q, R) system** is preferred because:
- These items represent 70–80% of inventory value
- A single stockout halts the assembly line
- The lower safety stock reduces capital tied up
- Real-time ERP tracking (SAP / Oracle) makes it feasible

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> *Each Part B question in the KTU ESE carries 14 marks. Typically, part (a) is worth 7 marks and part (b) is worth 7 marks. You must answer either OR question. Always write a definition (1 mark), formula (1 mark), substitution (2 marks), final answer with units (1 mark), and interpretation (2 marks) for any numerical part.*

---

### **Part B — Question A (14 Marks)**

#### **Q.A [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper Module 2]**

**(a)** A Tier-1 automotive supplier supplies **brake pads** to Tata Motors. The annual demand is **$D = 8{,}000$ units**, the ordering cost is **$S = \text{₹}\,2{,}000$ per order**, and the holding cost is **$H = \text{₹}\,40$ per unit per year**. Lead time is **$L = 6$ days**. Working days = **300/year**.

Calculate:
- (i) Economic Order Quantity (EOQ) **(3 marks)**
- (ii) Minimum total annual cost **(2 marks)**
- (iii) Number of orders per year and cycle time in days **(2 marks)**

#### **(b)** Explain **ABC analysis** with reference to an automotive assembly plant. State how inventory control policies differ for A, B, and C items. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a):**

**(i) EOQ Calculation (3 marks):**

$$Q^{*} = \sqrt{\frac{2DS}{H}} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 8000 \times 2000}{40}}$$

$$Q^{*} = \sqrt{\frac{32{,}000{,}000}{40}} = \sqrt{800{,}000}$$

$$\boxed{Q^{*} \approx 894.43 \approx 895 \text{ units}}$$

**(ii) Minimum Total Cost (2 marks):**

$$TC^{*} = \sqrt{2DSH} = \sqrt{2 \times 8000 \times 2000 \times 40}$$

$$TC^{*} = \sqrt{1{,}280{,}000{,}000} = \text{₹}\,35{,}777.09 \approx \text{₹}\,35{,}777$$

**(iii) Number of Orders and Cycle Time (2 marks):**

$$N = \frac{D}{Q^{*}} = \frac{8000}{894.43} \approx 8.94 \approx 9 \text{ orders/year}$$

$$T = \frac{Q^{*}}{D} \times 300 = \frac{894.43}{8000} \times 300 \approx 33.5 \text{ days/order}$$

---

**Part (b) — ABC Analysis (7 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):** ABC analysis is an inventory categorization technique based on the **Pareto principle (80/20 rule)**, which classifies items by their annual consumption value into three categories.

**Automotive Application (3 marks):**

| Class | % of SKUs | % of Value | Examples (Tata Motors) | Control Policy |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **A** | 15–20% | 70–80% | Engines, transmissions, ECUs, airbags | Continuous review (Q, R); tight tracking; daily review; low safety stock |
| **B** | 20–30% | 15–25% | Brake pads, alternators, headlamps | Periodic review (P); weekly check; moderate safety stock |
| **C** | 50–60% | 5–10% | Screws, washers, clips, grommets | Two-bin system; bulk ordering; loose tracking |

**Interpretation (2 marks):** ABC analysis enables **differential attention** — the firm invests managerial effort where it yields the highest cost savings. A class items get rigorous ERP-level tracking, B items get periodic review, and C items get bulk-purchase simplicity. This is the **same strategy used by Toyota, BMW, and Tata** in their plants.

---

### **Part B — Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

#### **Q.B [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Supplementary]**

**(a)** The daily demand for a **Mahindra OEM coolant** has mean $\mu_d = 50$ units and standard deviation $\sigma_d = 8$ units. Lead time has mean $L = 7$ days and standard deviation $\sigma_L = 1$ day. The firm wants a **97.5% cycle service level**.

Calculate:
- (i) Safety stock **(2 marks)**
- (ii) Reorder point **(2 marks)**
- (iii) Average inventory if EOQ = 600 units **(3 marks)**

#### **(b)** Discuss **Just-In-Time (JIT)** and **Vendor-Managed Inventory (VMI)** as optimization strategies for the automotive supply chain. Compare their advantages and limitations. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a):**

**(i) Safety Stock (2 marks):**
For 97.5% service level, $z = 1.96$.

$$\sigma_{LT} = \sqrt{L \cdot \sigma_{d}^{2} + d^{2} \cdot \sigma_{L}^{2}}$$

$$\sigma_{LT} = \sqrt{7 \times 8^{2} + 50^{2} \times 1^{2}} = \sqrt{448 + 2500} = \sqrt{2948}$$

$$\sigma_{LT} \approx 54.30 \text{ units}$$

$$SS = z \cdot \sigma_{LT} = 1.96 \times 54.30 \approx \boxed{106.43 \text{ units}}$$

**(ii) Reorder Point (2 marks):**

$$ROP = d \cdot L + SS = 50 \times 7 + 106.43 = 350 + 106.43$$

$$\boxed{ROP \approx 456.43 \approx 457 \text{ units}}$$

**(iii) Average Inventory (3 marks):**

$$\bar{I} = \frac{Q}{2} + SS = \frac{600}{2} + 106.43 = 300 + 106.43$$

$$\boxed{\bar{I} \approx 406.43 \text{ units}}$$

---

**Part (b) — JIT vs VMI (7 marks):**

**Just-In-Time (JIT) — Taichi Ohno, Toyota (2 marks):**
- Components arrive **exactly when needed** on the assembly line
- **Pull system** driven by Kanban cards
- **WIP inventory near zero** at each station (2–8 hours buffer)
- Requires **stable demand, reliable suppliers, and short lead times**

**Vendor-Managed Inventory (VMI) — 1990s retail-to-industry adoption (2 marks):**
- **Supplier** monitors OEM's stock and decides replenishment
- Common in Bosch, Continental → VW, Toyota, Mahindra
- Information asymmetry reduced; bullwhip dampened

**Comparison Table (2 marks):**

| Criterion | JIT | VMI |
|---|---|---|
| Inventory level | Very low | Moderate |
| Information flow | Kanban signal | Continuous EDI |
| Supplier power | Balanced | Supplier-led |
| Risk of stockout | High if disrupted | Lower |
| Best for | Repetitive high-volume | Variable demand |

**Conclusion (1 mark):** Modern automotive firms use a **hybrid approach** — JIT for high-volume, predictable sub-assemblies (seats, dashboards) and VMI for electronic and Tier-1 items with variable demand.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes KTU examiners penalize — avoid these to score full marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting units in the final answer** → Lose 0.5–1 mark. Always write "units" or "₹/year".
> 2. **Plugging into EOQ formula without verifying assumptions** (e.g., using EOQ for lumpy demand) → Lose 1 mark. State assumptions first.
> 3. **Confusing $H$ (holding cost per unit per year) with $i$ (carrying cost rate)** → $H = i \times C$, where $C$ is unit cost.
> 4. **Wrong $z$-value in safety stock problems** → For 95% use 1.65, NOT 1.96 (which is 97.5%). Examiners deduct 1 mark.
> 5. **Forgetting to convert lead time units** (days vs. years) → If $L$ is in days and $d$ is in units/day, keep units consistent.
> 6. **Writing "As per Wilson's formula" without deriving** → KTU's 14-mark questions specifically expect the **derivation steps** in part (a) when asked.
> 7. **Skipping the second-derivative test** in EOQ proofs → Examiners reserve 0.5 marks for verifying minimum.
> 8. **Mis-defining A/B/C items as % of SKUs vs. % of value** → This is the **most common conceptual error** in 3-mark Part A answers.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Print this section. Memorize it. Walk into the exam hall with confidence.**

### 📌 Core Concepts
- ✅ Inventory types: **RM, WIP, FG, MRO, Pipeline**
- ✅ Cost components: **Ordering, Holding, Shortage**
- ✅ Inventory purpose: **Decoupling, Buffering, Scale, Hedging**

### 📌 Must-Know Formulas
- ✅ $Q^{*} = \sqrt{2DS / H}$ (EOQ)
- ✅ $TC^{*} = \sqrt{2DSH}$
- ✅ $ROP = dL + z\sigma_{LT}$
- ✅ $SS = z\sigma_{LT}$
- ✅ $\bar{I} = Q/2 + SS$
- ✅ Inventory Turnover = COGS / Avg. Inventory

### 📌 ABC Analysis
- ✅ **A:** 20% SKUs, 80% value → tight control, continuous review
- ✅ **B:** 30% SKUs, 15% value → moderate control
- ✅ **C:** 50% SKUs, 5% value → loose control, bulk ordering

### 📌 Optimization Strategies
- ✅ **JIT (Toyota)** → pull, Kanban, low WIP
- ✅ **VMI** → supplier manages stock at buyer
- ✅ **Cross-docking** → no storage, fast transfer
- ✅ **Milk run** → single truck, multiple suppliers
- ✅ **Hub-and-spoke** → consolidated, cost-efficient
- ✅ **CPFR** → collaborative planning to reduce bullwhip

### 📌 Key Automotive Insights
- ✅ Line stoppage cost: **₹50–80 lakh/hour**
- ✅ Average car: **20,000+ parts**
- ✅ WIP at each station: **2–8 hours** in JIT plants
- ✅ Typical holding cost: **20–30% of item value/year**

### 📌 KTU 2024 Marks Strategy
- ✅ Part A (3 marks): Definition (1) + Explanation (2)
- ✅ Part B (14 marks): (a) 7 marks numerical + (b) 7 marks theory
- ✅ Always **show units**, **state assumptions**, and **interpret results**
- ✅ Use **headings and sub-points** — examiners reward structured answers

> 🎯 **Final Exam Tip:** When a Part B question offers a choice between EOQ/JIT/ABC/VMI theory and a numerical problem, choose the one you are most confident in. If you know the EOQ derivation cold, take that path — it scores the highest. Good luck!
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Production Planning and Control: Integration of Production Schedules with Supply Chain Processes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT753 - SUPPLY CHAIN MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Production Planning and Control: Integration of Production Schedules with Supply Chain Processes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

**Production Planning and Control (PPC)** is a scientific, integrated, and dynamic management function that plans, directs, and controls the **material flow, production capacity, and operational activities** of a manufacturing enterprise to ensure that the right quantity of the right product is produced at the right time, in the right quality, and at the right cost, in harmony with customer demand and the broader supply chain.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (verbatim formulation):**
> *"Production Planning and Control (PPC) is the orchestration of manufacturing resources, schedules, and shop-floor activities in synchronization with procurement, inventory, distribution, and demand signals across the supply chain to achieve delivery, cost, and service-level targets."*

The **integration of production schedules with supply chain processes** specifically refers to the seamless bidirectional coupling of:
- **Master Production Schedule (MPS)**
- **Material Requirements Planning (MRP)**
- **Shop Floor Execution (SFE)**
- with upstream **supplier networks** and downstream **distribution/logistics networks**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Orchestra Conductor:**
> Imagine a symphony orchestra. The **conductor** does not play every instrument, but coordinates the **violin section (suppliers)**, the **brass (production)**, the **woodwinds (inventory)**, and the **percussion (distribution)** to produce a single, harmonious sound (the finished product, on time).
> - The **score sheet** = the Master Production Schedule
> - The **conductor** = the PPC Manager
> - The **musicians** = supply chain partners (suppliers, warehouses, transporters)
> - The **audience** = the end customer
> - A **missed beat** in any section = a **bullwhip disruption** in the supply chain

The **Production Schedule** is essentially the **temporal blueprint** of *what* gets made, *how much*, *when*, and *by whom*, and its **integration with supply chain processes** ensures that the *raw material arrives before the work order opens*, the *finished good exits before the truck arrives*, and the *demand forecast matches the production capacity*.

### 1.3 Standard PPC Metrics (KTU High-Frequency Terms)

| Metric | Description | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| **Throughput Time** | Total time a unit spends in the system | Days / Hours |
| **Cycle Time** | Time between successive unit completions | Minutes / Hours |
| **Lead Time** | Time from order receipt to delivery | Days |
| **Takt Time** | Pace of production to match demand | Seconds / Unit |
| **Capacity Utilization** | Actual output ÷ Maximum possible output | **%** |
| **On-Time-In-Full (OTIF)** | Orders delivered on time and complete | **%** |
| **Work-In-Progress (WIP)** | Partially finished goods in system | Units / Value |
| **Set-up Time** | Time to prepare a machine for a new product | Minutes |

> [!NOTE]
> **Universal Truth in PPC:** Effective production planning is the **spinal cord** of supply chain management. Without a robust integration of production schedules with supplier deliveries and distribution plans, the entire supply chain suffers from the **bullwhip effect** — a phenomenon where small fluctuations in retail demand cause progressively larger oscillations upstream.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Pillars of PPC

PPC rests on three operational pillars:

1. **Planning (Forward-Looking):**
   - Demand forecasting
   - Aggregate Production Planning (APP)
   - Master Production Scheduling (MPS)
   - Material Requirements Planning (MRP)
   - Capacity Planning (Rough-Cut & Capacity Requirements Planning)

2. **Routing (Where):**
   - Determining the **path** of manufacture — operation sequence, machines, tooling
   - The Bill of Materials (BOM) explosion
   - Work center assignment

3. **Scheduling (When):**
   - **Loading:** Assigning work to work centers
   - **Sequencing:** Determining the order of jobs (FCFS, SPT, EDD, CR)
   - **Dispatching:** Releasing orders to the shop floor

> [!NOTE]
> **Valuation Insight:** KTU examiners often test the **differences** between Loading, Sequencing, and Dispatching. Memorize the order: **Load → Sequence → Dispatch**.

### 2.2 The Five Phases of Production Planning

| Phase | Function | Key Output |
|---|---|---|
| **Phase 1: Planning** | Aggregate planning & forecasting | Production Plan |
| **Phase 2: Routing** | Process & operation sequencing | Route Sheets |
| **Phase 3: Scheduling** | Time-phased work orders | Master Schedule |
| **Phase 4: Dispatching** | Order release authorization | Shop Orders |
| **Phase 5: Expediting / Follow-up** | Progress monitoring & correction | Control Reports |

### 2.3 Integration with Supply Chain Processes

Production scheduling does not operate in isolation. Its **integration points** with the broader supply chain include:

- **Upstream (Procurement Side):**
  - MPS feeds directly into **Purchase Requisitions**
  - Supplier Lead Times must be honored in MRP
  - **Vendor-Managed Inventory (VMI)** requires schedule visibility
  - **Just-In-Time (JIT)** delivery is anchored to production sequencing

- **Internal (Operations Side):**
  - Capacity must match the schedule (CRP)
  - **Shop Floor Control (SFC)** updates real-time progress
  - WIP inventory is monitored against plan

- **Downstream (Distribution Side):**
  - Finished Goods schedules feed **Warehouse Management Systems (WMS)**
  - **Available-to-Promise (ATP)** logic requires synchronization
  - **Customer Order Decoupling Point (CODP)** determines make-to-stock vs. make-to-order boundary

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Production Planning & Scheduling)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL KTU FORMULAS** — Frequently tested in 3-mark and 14-mark problems.

| # | Formula | Description | Variables / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $$EBQ = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \cdot D \cdot S}{H}}$$ | **Economic Batch Quantity** (EOQ for batches) | $D$ = Annual demand (units), $S$ = Setup cost per batch (\$), $H$ = Holding cost per unit per year (\$/unit-yr) |
| 2 | $$TC = \underbrace{\dfrac{D}{Q} \cdot S}_{\text{Setup Cost}} + \underbrace{\dfrac{Q}{2} \cdot H}_{\text{Holding Cost}}$$ | **Total Inventory Cost** | $Q$ = Order / batch quantity |
| 3 | $$N = \dfrac{D}{Q}$$ | **Number of orders / setups per year** | Dimensionless |
| 4 | $$T_{cycle} = \dfrac{\text{Working Days per Year}}{N} = \dfrac{Q}{D} \cdot 365$$ | **Cycle Time (days)** | Days |
| 5 | $$R = \bar{d} \cdot L$$ | **Reorder Point** | $\bar{d}$ = Avg daily demand, $L$ = Lead time (days) |
| 6 | $$\text{Capacity Utilization} = \dfrac{\text{Actual Output}}{\text{Designed Capacity}} \times 100\%$$ | **Capacity utilization ratio** | Percentage |
| 7 | $$\text{Takt Time} = \dfrac{\text{Available Production Time}}{\text{Customer Demand}}$$ | **Customer-paced production rate** | Time per unit |
| 8 | $$\text{OTIF} = \dfrac{\text{Orders Delivered On Time \& In Full}}{\text{Total Orders Shipped}} \times 100\%$$ | **Service-level metric** | Percentage |
| 9 | $$\text{Work-In-Progress (WIP)} = \text{TH} \cdot CT$$ | **Little's Law** | $TH$ = Throughput rate, $CT$ = Cycle time |
| 10 | $$\text{Production Cost/Unit} = \dfrac{F_c + V_c \cdot Q}{Q}$$ | **Unit production cost** | $F_c$ = Fixed cost, $V_c$ = Variable cost/unit |

> [!NOTE]
> **Formula Note:** In markdown tables, the **vertical pipe** `|` has been replaced with the LaTeX commands `\vert` or `\mid` to prevent table-rendering failures. Always do this in KTU digital submissions.

### 2.5 Real-World Utility
